details.etymology: Compound of Flug (flight) and Hafen (harbor/port), literally meaning 'flight harbor,' coined in the early 20th century as aviation developed.
examples: Wir müssen um sechs Uhr am Flughafen sein. (We have to be at the airport at six o'clock.)
Der Flughafen ist weit vom Stadtzentrum entfernt. (The airport is far from the city center.)
details.usage: Commonly used in compounds like Flughafenbus (airport bus) and Flughafentransfer (airport transfer).

details.etymology: Compound of Bahn (track/railway) and Hof (yard/court), originally referring to the yard where trains were kept.
examples: Der Zug fährt vom Hauptbahnhof ab. (The train departs from the main station.)
Kannst du mich am Bahnhof abholen? (Can you pick me up at the train station?)
details.usage: The idiom 'Ich verstehe nur Bahnhof' means 'I don't understand anything,' and Hauptbahnhof (Hbf) refers to the main station.

details.etymology: Compound of krank (sick) and Haus (house), literally 'house for the sick,' used since the Middle Ages.
examples: Er wurde ins Krankenhaus gebracht. (He was taken to the hospital.)
Das Krankenhaus hat eine neue Abteilung eröffnet. (The hospital opened a new department.)
details.usage: In Austria, Spital is more commonly used; Krankenhaus is standard in Germany and forms compounds like Krankenhausaufenthalt (hospital stay).

details.etymology: Compound of aus (out) and Flug (flight), originally meaning 'flying out,' used figuratively for an outing or excursion since the 18th century.
examples: Am Wochenende machen wir einen Ausflug an den See. (On the weekend we are taking a trip to the lake.)
Der Ausflug in die Berge war wunderschön. (The excursion to the mountains was wonderful.)
details.usage: Often used for day trips and school outings (Schulausflug), and commonly paired with machen (einen Ausflug machen).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'nachrihte,' meaning 'something to be guided by,' derived from 'nach' (after) and 'richten' (to direct).
examples: Hast du meine Nachricht gelesen? (Did you read my message?)
Die Nachrichten kommen um acht Uhr. (The news comes on at eight o'clock.)
details.usage: In the plural, Nachrichten commonly means 'the news' (TV/radio), while the singular means a single message or piece of news.

details.etymology: Derived from 'erfahren' (to experience/learn), from Middle High German 'ervarn' meaning 'to travel through' or 'to find out.'
examples: Sie hat viel Erfahrung in diesem Beruf. (She has a lot of experience in this profession.)
Das war eine gute Erfahrung für mich. (That was a good experience for me.)
details.usage: Commonly used in job contexts (Berufserfahrung = professional experience) and in the phrase 'aus Erfahrung' (from experience).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gewiter,' related to 'Wetter' (weather), with the collective prefix 'ge-' indicating an intense weather event.
examples: Heute Nacht soll es ein starkes Gewitter geben. (Tonight there is supposed to be a strong thunderstorm.)
Das Gewitter hat zwei Stunden gedauert. (The thunderstorm lasted two hours.)
details.usage: The plural is identical to the singular; it is often used with 'es gibt' (es gibt ein Gewitter) and appears in Gewitterwarnung (thunderstorm warning).

details.etymology: Compound of Geburt (birth) and Tag (day), literally 'birth day,' used in German since the 16th century.
examples: Herzlichen Glückwunsch zum Geburtstag! (Happy birthday!)
Mein Geburtstag ist im Mai. (My birthday is in May.)
details.usage: In Germany, it is considered bad luck to wish someone happy birthday before the actual day; common compound is Geburtstagsfeier (birthday party).

details.etymology: Compound of früh (early) and Stück (piece), originally meaning 'early piece of bread,' referring to the first meal of the day.
examples: Was möchtest du zum Frühstück? (What would you like for breakfast?)
Das Frühstück ist im Hotelpreis inbegriffen. (Breakfast is included in the hotel price.)
details.usage: A typical German Frühstück includes bread, cold cuts, cheese, and jam; the verb form is frühstücken.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'antwurti,' related to 'Wort' (word), literally meaning 'a word given in return.'
examples: Ich warte auf deine Antwort. (I am waiting for your answer.)
Die Antwort auf die Frage war falsch. (The answer to the question was wrong.)
details.usage: Used with the verb 'antworten' (to answer) and in the phrase 'eine Antwort geben' (to give an answer).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'bīspel,' a compound of 'bei' (by/near) and 'spel' (story/tale), originally meaning 'an illustrative story.'
examples: Können Sie mir ein Beispiel geben? (Can you give me an example?)
Zum Beispiel esse ich gern Pizza. (For example, I like to eat pizza.)
details.usage: The abbreviation 'z.B.' (zum Beispiel) meaning 'for example' is extremely common in written German.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'urloup,' meaning 'permission to leave,' originally used in a military context for leave of absence.
examples: Wir fahren im Sommer in den Urlaub. (We are going on vacation in the summer.)
Er hat drei Wochen Urlaub genommen. (He took three weeks of vacation.)
details.usage: In Germany, Urlaub refers to vacation time from work, while Ferien refers to school holidays; common compound is Urlaubstag (vacation day).

details.etymology: Derived from 'ordnen' (to order/arrange), from Latin 'ordo' via Old High German, meaning 'arrangement' or 'order.'
examples: Alles ist in Ordnung. (Everything is in order.)
Ordnung muss sein! (There must be order!)
details.usage: The phrase 'Ordnung muss sein' is a well-known German cultural saying reflecting the value placed on orderliness.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'vēler,' derived from 'fehlen' (to be missing/to err), originally meaning 'a lack or deficiency.'
examples: Jeder macht mal Fehler. (Everyone makes mistakes sometimes.)
In dem Text sind viele Fehler. (There are many errors in the text.)
details.usage: The plural is the same as the singular; commonly used in compounds like Rechtschreibfehler (spelling mistake) and Fehlerquote (error rate).

details.etymology: Derived from 'lösen' (to solve/dissolve), from Old High German 'lōsen,' meaning both a solution to a problem and a dissolved substance.
examples: Wir müssen eine Lösung finden. (We have to find a solution.)
Die Lösung des Problems war einfach. (The solution to the problem was simple.)
details.usage: Has a dual meaning: a solution to a problem and a chemical solution (Salzlösung = saline solution).

details.etymology: Derived from 'fühlen' (to feel) with the prefix 'ge-,' from Middle High German 'gevüele,' meaning 'feeling' or 'sensation.'
examples: Ich habe ein gutes Gefühl dabei. (I have a good feeling about it.)
Man sollte über seine Gefühle sprechen. (One should talk about one's feelings.)
details.usage: Often used in the phrase 'ein Gefühl haben' (to have a feeling) and in compounds like Bauchgefühl (gut feeling).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hungar,' related to English 'hunger,' from Proto-Germanic *hungruz, one of the oldest words shared across Germanic languages.
examples: Ich habe großen Hunger. (I am very hungry.)
Hunger ist der beste Koch. (Hunger is the best cook.)
details.usage: Typically used without a plural; the proverb 'Hunger ist der beste Koch' means everything tastes good when you are hungry.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kuhhina,' borrowed from Latin 'coquina' (a place for cooking), related to 'coquere' (to cook).
examples: Die Küche ist frisch renoviert. (The kitchen has been freshly renovated.)
Die deutsche Küche ist bekannt für Würstchen und Brot. (German cuisine is known for sausages and bread.)
details.usage: Refers both to the room (kitchen) and to a style of cooking (cuisine), as in 'die französische Küche' (French cuisine).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'brucka,' related to Proto-Germanic *brugjo, originally meaning a log or plank laid across water.
examples: Die alte Brücke über den Fluss ist sehr schön. (The old bridge over the river is very beautiful.)
Wir müssen über die Brücke gehen. (We have to go across the bridge.)
details.usage: Also used figuratively to mean 'bridge' between people or ideas; a Brückentag is a day off taken between a holiday and a weekend.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'nāhgibūr,' a compound of 'nāh' (near) and 'gibūr' (farmer/dweller), literally meaning 'near dweller.'
examples: Unser Nachbar ist sehr freundlich. (Our neighbor is very friendly.)
Die Nachbarn haben sich über den Lärm beschwert. (The neighbors complained about the noise.)
details.usage: The feminine form is Nachbarin; common compounds include Nachbarschaft (neighborhood) and Nachbarland (neighboring country).

details.etymology: Derived from 'packen' (to pack) with the collective prefix 'ge-,' meaning 'that which has been packed,' used since the 16th century.
examples: Wo kann ich mein Gepäck abgeben? (Where can I drop off my luggage?)
Das Gepäck ist zu schwer. (The luggage is too heavy.)
details.usage: A collective noun with no standard plural; common compounds include Handgepäck (hand luggage) and Gepäckband (baggage carousel).

details.etymology: Derived from 'rechnen' (to calculate), from Old High German 'rehhanōn,' meaning 'a calculation' or 'an invoice.'
examples: Kann ich bitte die Rechnung haben? (Can I have the bill, please?)
Die Rechnung stimmt nicht. (The bill is not correct.)
details.usage: In restaurants, asking for 'die Rechnung' is the standard way to request the bill; also means 'invoice' in business contexts.

details.etymology: Derived from 'wohnen' (to live/reside), from Old High German 'wonēn,' meaning 'a dwelling' or 'apartment.'
examples: Die Wohnung hat drei Zimmer. (The apartment has three rooms.)
Wir suchen eine neue Wohnung. (We are looking for a new apartment.)
details.usage: In German-speaking countries, most people rent their Wohnung; common compounds include Mietwohnung (rental apartment) and Eigentumswohnung (condominium).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'anafang,' a compound of 'an' (at/on) and 'fang' (catch/beginning), meaning 'the beginning or start.'
examples: Am Anfang war es schwierig. (In the beginning it was difficult.)
Aller Anfang ist schwer. (Every beginning is hard.)
details.usage: The proverb 'Aller Anfang ist schwer' is widely used to encourage someone starting something new.

details.etymology: From Latin 'speculum' (mirror) via Old High German 'spiegal,' adopted during the Roman period.
examples: Sie schaut jeden Morgen in den Spiegel. (She looks in the mirror every morning.)
Der Spiegel an der Wand ist zerbrochen. (The mirror on the wall is broken.)
details.usage: Also the name of the famous German news magazine 'Der Spiegel'; compounds include Rückspiegel (rearview mirror) and Spiegelei (fried egg).

details.etymology: Compound of 'ab' (off/away) and 'Fahrt' (journey/drive), meaning 'departure' or 'the act of driving away.'
examples: Die Abfahrt des Zuges ist um 14 Uhr. (The train's departure is at 2 PM.)
Bitte beachten Sie die Abfahrtszeiten. (Please note the departure times.)
details.usage: Used for trains, buses, and also in skiing where Abfahrt means 'downhill run'; the departure board is called Abfahrtstafel.

details.etymology: Compound of 'an' (at/to) and 'Kunft' (coming, from 'kommen'), meaning 'arrival,' used since Middle High German.
examples: Die Ankunft des Fluges ist um 10 Uhr. (The flight's arrival is at 10 o'clock.)
Nach der Ankunft im Hotel haben wir uns ausgeruht. (After arriving at the hotel, we rested.)
details.usage: Commonly seen on airport and train station signs alongside Abfahrt; Ankunftszeit means 'arrival time.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'aus' (out) and 'Gang' (way/passage), literally meaning 'the way out,' from Middle High German.
examples: Wo ist der Ausgang? (Where is the exit?)
Der Notausgang ist rechts. (The emergency exit is on the right.)
details.usage: Notausgang (emergency exit) is an important compound; Ausgang also means 'outcome' in figurative use.

details.etymology: Compound of 'ein' (in) and 'Gang' (way/passage), literally meaning 'the way in,' the counterpart to Ausgang.
examples: Der Eingang ist auf der anderen Seite. (The entrance is on the other side.)
Am Eingang steht ein Sicherheitsmann. (A security guard stands at the entrance.)
details.usage: Haupteingang means 'main entrance'; Eingang is also used figuratively for 'receipt' in business (Eingang einer E-Mail).

details.etymology: Compound of 'auf' (up) and 'Zug' (pull/movement), literally 'something that pulls up,' referring to a lift or elevator.
examples: Nehmen wir den Aufzug oder die Treppe? (Shall we take the elevator or the stairs?)
Der Aufzug ist leider außer Betrieb. (The elevator is unfortunately out of order.)
details.usage: In northern Germany, Aufzug is common, while in southern Germany and Austria, Lift (from English) is often preferred.

details.etymology: Compound of 'aus' (out) and 'Sicht' (sight/view), meaning 'the view outward' or 'prospect.'
examples: Von hier hat man eine tolle Aussicht. (From here you have a great view.)
Die Aussichten auf dem Arbeitsmarkt sind gut. (The prospects in the job market are good.)
details.usage: Used both literally (scenic view) and figuratively (prospects/outlook); Aussichtsturm means 'observation tower.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'berīch,' related to 'Reich' (realm/empire), meaning 'domain' or 'area.'
examples: In welchem Bereich arbeiten Sie? (In which field do you work?)
Dieser Bereich ist für Mitarbeiter reserviert. (This area is reserved for employees.)
details.usage: Widely used in professional contexts meaning 'field' or 'sector,' as well as physically for 'area' or 'zone.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'besuchen' (to visit), from Old High German 'bisuohhen,' meaning 'to seek out someone.'
examples: Wir bekommen morgen Besuch. (We are getting visitors tomorrow.)
Der Besuch im Museum war sehr interessant. (The visit to the museum was very interesting.)
details.usage: Can mean both 'a visit' and 'visitors' (as a collective noun); zu Besuch sein means 'to be visiting.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'betragen' (to amount to), from Middle High German, meaning 'the amount' something adds up to.
examples: Der Betrag auf der Rechnung ist falsch. (The amount on the bill is wrong.)
Bitte überweisen Sie den Betrag bis Freitag. (Please transfer the amount by Friday.)
details.usage: Primarily used in financial and banking contexts; Gesamtbetrag means 'total amount.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'bilden' (to form/educate), from 'Bild' (image), originally meaning 'forming an image' and later 'education.'
examples: Bildung ist sehr wichtig für die Zukunft. (Education is very important for the future.)
Er hat eine gute Bildung genossen. (He received a good education.)
details.usage: Bildung carries a deeper meaning than just schooling, encompassing cultural and personal development; Bildungssystem means 'education system.'

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'bureau' (office/desk), which itself came from Old French 'burel' (coarse woolen cloth used to cover desks).
examples: Ich arbeite jeden Tag im Büro. (I work in the office every day.)
Das Büro ist im dritten Stock. (The office is on the third floor.)
details.usage: Common in modern work life; compounds include Bürogebäude (office building) and Reisebüro (travel agency).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'dūmo,' related to Proto-Germanic *þūmô and Latin 'tumere' (to swell), referring to the 'thick finger.'
examples: Ich drücke dir die Daumen! (I'll keep my fingers crossed for you!)
Er hat sich den Daumen gebrochen. (He broke his thumb.)
details.usage: The phrase 'die Daumen drücken' (to press the thumbs) is the German equivalent of 'crossing fingers' for good luck.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'decchī,' related to 'decken' (to cover), meaning both 'blanket' (something that covers) and 'ceiling.'
examples: Die Decke in diesem Zimmer ist sehr hoch. (The ceiling in this room is very high.)
Kannst du mir eine Decke bringen? (Can you bring me a blanket?)
details.usage: Has two distinct meanings: 'ceiling' and 'blanket'; the idiom 'mir fällt die Decke auf den Kopf' means 'I'm going stir-crazy.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'drucken' (to print) with the agent suffix '-er,' meaning 'printer,' from early modern German printing technology.
examples: Der Drucker funktioniert nicht. (The printer is not working.)
Wir brauchen einen neuen Drucker fürs Büro. (We need a new printer for the office.)
details.usage: Can refer to both a machine (printer) and a person (printer/typographer); Laserdrucker and Tintenstrahldrucker are common types.

context: Der ___ ist groß nach dem Sport.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'durst,' related to Proto-Germanic *þurstuz and English 'thirst,' one of the basic Germanic vocabulary words.
examples: Ich habe großen Durst. (I am very thirsty.)
Nach dem Sport hat man immer Durst. (After sports, you are always thirsty.)
details.usage: Like Hunger, Durst is used without a plural and in the construction 'Durst haben' (to be thirsty).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ecka,' related to Latin 'acies' (sharp edge), originally meaning 'sharp point' or 'corner.'
examples: Das Café ist gleich um die Ecke. (The café is just around the corner.)
Stell den Schrank in die Ecke. (Put the wardrobe in the corner.)
details.usage: The phrase 'um die Ecke' (around the corner) is very common; also used in soccer for Eckball (corner kick).

details.etymology: Derived from 'einladen' (to invite), compound of 'ein' (in) and 'laden' (to load/invite), originally meaning 'to load in' as a guest.
examples: Vielen Dank für die Einladung! (Thank you very much for the invitation!)
Hast du die Einladung zur Party bekommen? (Did you get the invitation to the party?)
details.usage: Commonly used for formal and informal invitations; Einladungskarte means 'invitation card.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'empfangen' (to receive), from Old High German 'intfāhan,' meaning 'reception' in various senses.
examples: Bitte melden Sie sich am Empfang. (Please check in at the reception.)
Der Empfang in diesem Hotel war sehr freundlich. (The reception at this hotel was very friendly.)
details.usage: Used for hotel/office reception desks, TV/radio signal reception, and formal reception events.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Erde' (earth/ground) and 'Geschoss' (floor/story), literally meaning 'ground floor.'
examples: Die Wohnung liegt im Erdgeschoss. (The apartment is on the ground floor.)
Im Erdgeschoss gibt es ein Café. (There is a café on the ground floor.)
details.usage: In German counting, Erdgeschoss is floor 0, and the first floor above is 'erster Stock' or 'erste Etage,' differing from American convention.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'erfolg,' derived from 'erfolgen' (to follow/result), meaning 'what follows from an effort' — i.e., success.
examples: Ich wünsche dir viel Erfolg! (I wish you much success!)
Der Film war ein großer Erfolg. (The film was a big success.)
details.usage: Erfolgreich means 'successful'; compounds include Erfolgsgeschichte (success story) and Erfolgserlebnis (sense of achievement).

details.etymology: Derived from 'erinnern' (to remind/remember), from Middle High German 'erinnern,' meaning 'to bring inward again.'
examples: Ich habe schöne Erinnerungen an meine Kindheit. (I have beautiful memories of my childhood.)
Das Foto ist eine Erinnerung an unseren Urlaub. (The photo is a memento of our vacation.)
details.usage: Can mean 'memory,' 'reminder,' or 'souvenir'; Erinnerungsstück means 'keepsake.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'erlauben' (to permit/allow), from Old High German 'irlouben,' meaning 'permission.'
examples: Haben Sie die Erlaubnis, hier zu parken? (Do you have permission to park here?)
Ohne Erlaubnis darf man das Gebäude nicht betreten. (Without permission, you may not enter the building.)
details.usage: Used in formal and legal contexts; related words include Arbeitserlaubnis (work permit) and Aufenthaltserlaubnis (residence permit).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'fabrique,' from Latin 'fabrica' (workshop), related to 'faber' (craftsman).
examples: In der Fabrik arbeiten 500 Menschen. (500 people work in the factory.)
Die alte Fabrik wurde zu einem Museum umgebaut. (The old factory was converted into a museum.)
details.usage: Common in industrial contexts; compounds include Fabrikarbeiter (factory worker) and Schokoladenfabrik (chocolate factory).

details.etymology: Compound of 'fahren' (to travel/drive) and 'Karte' (card/ticket), literally meaning 'travel card.'
examples: Wo kann ich eine Fahrkarte kaufen? (Where can I buy a ticket?)
Die Fahrkarte gilt für zwei Zonen. (The ticket is valid for two zones.)
details.usage: Used specifically for transport tickets; Fahrkartenautomat is the ticket machine at stations.

details.etymology: Compound of 'fahren' (to travel) and 'Plan' (plan/schedule), literally meaning 'travel plan.'
examples: Schau mal im Fahrplan nach. (Have a look in the timetable.)
Der Fahrplan hat sich geändert. (The timetable has changed.)
details.usage: Refers to bus, train, and public transport schedules; fahrplanmäßig means 'on schedule.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'fahren' (to drive/ride) and 'Rad' (wheel), literally 'driving wheel,' coined in the 19th century.
examples: Ich fahre jeden Tag mit dem Fahrrad zur Arbeit. (I ride my bike to work every day.)
Mein Fahrrad hat einen Platten. (My bicycle has a flat tire.)
details.usage: Often shortened to 'Rad' in casual speech; Germany is a cycling-friendly country with dedicated Fahrradwege (bike paths).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fīra,' borrowed from Latin 'feria' (holiday/rest day), originally meaning a day of rest or celebration.
examples: Die Feier war sehr schön. (The celebration was very nice.)
Wir planen eine kleine Feier zum Jubiläum. (We are planning a small celebration for the anniversary.)
details.usage: Related words include Feiertag (holiday), feiern (to celebrate), and Feierabend (end of the work day).

details.etymology: From Latin 'febris' (fever) via Old High German 'fiebar,' one of the early medical Latin borrowings into German.
examples: Das Kind hat hohes Fieber. (The child has a high fever.)
Bei Fieber sollte man im Bett bleiben. (When you have a fever, you should stay in bed.)
details.usage: Typically used without a plural; Fieber messen means 'to take one's temperature,' and Fieberthermometer is a fever thermometer.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'vlæche,' related to 'flach' (flat), meaning 'a flat surface or area.'
examples: Die Fläche des Grundstücks beträgt 200 Quadratmeter. (The area of the property is 200 square meters.)
Die glatte Fläche eignet sich gut zum Schreiben. (The smooth surface is suitable for writing.)
details.usage: Used in geography, mathematics, and everyday life; Oberfläche means 'surface' and Wohnfläche means 'living space.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'flug,' derived from 'fliegen' (to fly), meaning 'flight' or 'the act of flying.'
examples: Der Flug nach Berlin dauert eine Stunde. (The flight to Berlin takes one hour.)
Unser Flug hat Verspätung. (Our flight is delayed.)
details.usage: Used in many compounds: Flugzeug (airplane), Flughafen (airport), Fluggesellschaft (airline), and Flugticket (flight ticket).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'formularium,' derived from 'formula' (a small form/pattern), entering German through administrative language.
examples: Bitte füllen Sie dieses Formular aus. (Please fill out this form.)
Das Formular muss unterschrieben werden. (The form must be signed.)
details.usage: Very common in bureaucratic Germany; ein Formular ausfüllen (to fill out a form) is an essential phrase for daily life.

details.etymology: Compound of 'fort' (forward/onward) and 'Schritt' (step), literally meaning 'a step forward,' i.e., progress.
examples: Die Medizin hat große Fortschritte gemacht. (Medicine has made great progress.)
Ich sehe schon Fortschritte bei deinem Deutsch. (I can already see progress in your German.)
details.usage: Often used in the plural (Fortschritte machen = to make progress); fortschrittlich means 'progressive.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'führen' (to lead/guide), from Old High German 'fuoren,' meaning 'guidance,' 'leadership,' or 'guided tour.'
examples: Um 15 Uhr beginnt die Führung durch das Schloss. (The guided tour through the castle begins at 3 PM.)
Unter seiner Führung hat die Firma Erfolg gehabt. (Under his leadership, the company was successful.)
details.usage: Can mean a guided tour (Museumsführung), leadership, or management depending on context.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'garde-robe' (wardrobe), literally 'guard-robe,' originally meaning a room for storing clothes.
examples: Bitte geben Sie Ihren Mantel an der Garderobe ab. (Please leave your coat at the cloakroom.)
Die Garderobe befindet sich im Erdgeschoss. (The cloakroom is on the ground floor.)
details.usage: In Germany, Garderobe means both 'cloakroom' (in theaters, restaurants) and a 'coat rack' at home.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gegent,' derived from 'gegen' (against/toward), originally meaning 'the area facing or opposite.'
examples: In dieser Gegend gibt es viele Restaurants. (There are many restaurants in this area.)
Die Gegend um den See ist wunderschön. (The area around the lake is beautiful.)
details.usage: A somewhat informal word for 'area' or 'region,' often used to describe neighborhoods or surroundings.

details.etymology: Derived from 'gelegen' (situated/convenient), meaning 'an occasion that is conveniently situated,' i.e., an opportunity.
examples: Das ist eine gute Gelegenheit zum Üben. (That is a good opportunity to practice.)
Bei Gelegenheit sollten wir uns treffen. (When the opportunity arises, we should meet.)
details.usage: The proverb 'Gelegenheit macht Diebe' (Opportunity makes thieves) is well known; bei Gelegenheit means 'when there is a chance.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'geschefte,' derived from 'schaffen' (to create/work), meaning 'business dealings' or 'a shop.'
examples: Das Geschäft hat bis 20 Uhr geöffnet. (The shop is open until 8 PM.)
Er macht gute Geschäfte. (He is doing good business.)
details.usage: Means both 'shop/store' and 'business/deal'; Geschäftsführer means 'managing director' and Geschäftsreise means 'business trip.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'schenken' (to give as a gift), from Old High German 'scenken' (to pour/serve), originally referring to offering a drink.
examples: Ich habe ein Geschenk für dich. (I have a gift for you.)
Was wünschst du dir als Geschenk? (What do you want as a gift?)
details.usage: Common compounds include Geburtstagsgeschenk (birthday present) and Weihnachtsgeschenk (Christmas present).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'geschiht,' related to 'geschehen' (to happen), meaning both 'story' (something that happened) and 'history.'
examples: Erzähl mir eine Geschichte! (Tell me a story!)
Geschichte war mein Lieblingsfach in der Schule. (History was my favorite subject in school.)
details.usage: Has a dual meaning: 'story/tale' and 'history' as an academic discipline; context determines which meaning is intended.

details.etymology: Derived from 'gesund' (healthy) with the abstract suffix '-heit,' from Old High German 'gisunt,' meaning 'the state of being healthy.'
examples: Gesundheit! (Bless you! — said after someone sneezes)
Die Gesundheit ist das Wichtigste. (Health is the most important thing.)
details.usage: Famously said after someone sneezes; also used in the toast 'Auf Ihre Gesundheit!' (To your health!); Gesundheitssystem means 'healthcare system.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gelücke,' possibly related to 'gelingen' (to succeed), meaning both 'luck' and 'happiness.'
examples: Viel Glück bei der Prüfung! (Good luck on the exam!)
Er hat großes Glück gehabt. (He was very lucky.)
details.usage: Uniquely means both 'luck' and 'happiness' in German; Glücksbringer is a 'lucky charm' and glücklich means 'happy.'

details.etymology: Borrowed from Polish 'granica' or Czech 'hranice' in the Middle Ages, replacing the older Germanic word 'Mark.'
examples: Die Grenze zwischen Deutschland und Österreich ist offen. (The border between Germany and Austria is open.)
Alles hat seine Grenzen. (Everything has its limits.)
details.usage: Used both literally for borders and figuratively for limits; Grenzkontrolle means 'border control.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'grunt,' meaning 'ground' or 'bottom,' later extended to mean 'reason' or 'cause.'
examples: Aus diesem Grund bin ich hier. (For this reason I am here.)
Es gibt keinen Grund zur Sorge. (There is no reason to worry.)
details.usage: Means both 'reason' and 'ground/land'; compounds include Grundstück (plot of land) and Grundschule (primary school).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hafen,' related to 'haben' (to have/hold), originally meaning 'a place that holds ships.'
examples: Das Schiff liegt im Hafen. (The ship is in the harbor.)
Hamburg hat einen der größten Häfen Europas. (Hamburg has one of the largest harbors in Europe.)
details.usage: Hamburg's port is famous; common compounds include Flughafen (airport) and Hafenstadt (port city).

details.etymology: Compound of 'halten' (to stop) and 'Stelle' (place), literally meaning 'stopping place.'
examples: Die nächste Haltestelle ist am Marktplatz. (The next stop is at the market square.)
Wir müssen an der nächsten Haltestelle aussteigen. (We have to get off at the next stop.)
details.usage: Used for bus and tram stops; Bushaltestelle (bus stop) and Straßenbahnhaltestelle (tram stop) are common compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Hand' (hand) and 'Tuch' (cloth), literally 'hand cloth,' originally a cloth for drying hands.
examples: Ich brauche ein sauberes Handtuch. (I need a clean towel.)
Die Handtücher liegen im Bad. (The towels are in the bathroom.)
details.usage: The stereotype of Germans reserving beach chairs with towels (Handtuch-Reservierung) is a well-known cultural joke.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'heimōti,' derived from 'Heim' (home), meaning 'homeland' or 'the place one feels at home.'
examples: Deutschland ist meine Heimat. (Germany is my homeland.)
Er vermisst seine Heimat. (He misses his homeland.)
details.usage: Heimat is a deeply emotional German concept with no exact English equivalent; it encompasses home, belonging, and cultural identity.

details.etymology: Derived from 'heizen' (to heat), from Old High German 'heizen,' meaning 'heating' or 'a heating system.'
examples: Die Heizung funktioniert nicht richtig. (The heating is not working properly.)
Kannst du bitte die Heizung aufdrehen? (Can you please turn up the heating?)
details.usage: Essential in German homes during winter; Zentralheizung means 'central heating' and Fußbodenheizung means 'underfloor heating.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'hin' (toward) and 'Weis' (from 'weisen,' to point/show), meaning 'an indication' or 'a tip.'
examples: Vielen Dank für den Hinweis. (Thank you very much for the tip.)
Bitte beachten Sie den folgenden Hinweis. (Please note the following notice.)
details.usage: Common on signs and in official communications; Hinweisschild means 'information sign.'

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'hobby' in the 20th century, which itself derives from 'hobbyhorse,' a small horse for leisure riding.
examples: Mein Hobby ist Fotografieren. (My hobby is photography.)
Hast du ein Hobby? (Do you have a hobby?)
details.usage: A common loanword in German; the plural follows German adaptation rules with -s (Hobbys, not Hobbies).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'huosto,' an onomatopoetic word imitating the sound of coughing, related to English 'cough' via Proto-Germanic.
examples: Er hat einen starken Husten. (He has a bad cough.)
Der Husten geht einfach nicht weg. (The cough just won't go away.)
details.usage: Used both as a noun (der Husten) and verb (husten = to cough); Hustensaft means 'cough syrup.'

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'idée,' ultimately from Greek 'idea' (form/notion), entering German in the 17th century.
examples: Das ist eine gute Idee! (That is a good idea!)
Hast du eine Idee, was wir machen können? (Do you have an idea of what we can do?)
details.usage: The colloquial phrase 'keine Idee' can mean 'no idea/no clue'; used in everyday conversation very frequently.

details.etymology: From Latin 'insula' (island) via Old High German 'insela,' replacing the older Germanic word for island.
examples: Wir machen Urlaub auf einer Insel. (We are vacationing on an island.)
Rügen ist die größte deutsche Insel. (Rügen is the largest German island.)
details.usage: Compounds include Halbinsel (peninsula) and Verkehrsinsel (traffic island); Germany's North Sea and Baltic islands are popular holiday destinations.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Jahr' (year) and 'Zeit' (time), with the genitive -es- linking element, literally meaning 'time of the year.'
examples: Der Frühling ist meine liebste Jahreszeit. (Spring is my favorite season.)
Jede Jahreszeit hat ihren Reiz. (Every season has its charm.)
details.usage: Germany has four distinct Jahreszeiten: Frühling (spring), Sommer (summer), Herbst (autumn), and Winter (winter).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'jugund,' related to 'jung' (young), meaning 'youth' as both a period of life and young people collectively.
examples: In meiner Jugend habe ich viel Sport gemacht. (In my youth I did a lot of sports.)
Die Jugend von heute ist sehr technikaffin. (Today's youth is very tech-savvy.)
details.usage: Used without a plural; compounds include Jugendherberge (youth hostel), Jugendliche (young person), and Jugendzentrum (youth center).

details.etymology: From Italian 'cassa' (box/chest), ultimately from Latin 'capsa' (container), entering German through medieval trade.
examples: Bitte zahlen Sie an der Kasse. (Please pay at the cash register.)
An der Kasse war eine lange Schlange. (There was a long line at the checkout.)
details.usage: Means both 'cash register' and 'cashier area'; Krankenkasse means 'health insurance fund' and Sparkasse means 'savings bank.'

details.etymology: From Latin 'cellarium' (storeroom/pantry) via Old High German 'kellari,' borrowed during Roman times.
examples: Der Wein lagert im Keller. (The wine is stored in the cellar.)
Unser Keller ist sehr feucht. (Our basement is very damp.)
details.usage: German houses traditionally have a Keller for storage; compounds include Weinkeller (wine cellar) and Kellergeschoss (basement floor).

details.etymology: Derived from 'kennen' (to know/be acquainted with), from Old High German 'kennan,' meaning 'knowledge' or 'awareness.'
examples: Haben Sie Kenntnisse in Buchhaltung? (Do you have knowledge of accounting?)
Ich habe davon keine Kenntnis. (I have no knowledge of that.)
details.usage: Often used in the plural for skills on a CV (Sprachkenntnisse = language skills); zur Kenntnis nehmen means 'to take note of.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kussin,' borrowed from Latin 'cussinus' (cushion), related to French 'coussin.'
examples: Dieses Kissen ist sehr weich. (This pillow is very soft.)
Leg dir ein Kissen unter den Kopf. (Put a pillow under your head.)
details.usage: Can mean both 'pillow' and 'cushion'; Kopfkissen is specifically a bed pillow and Sofakissen is a sofa cushion.

details.etymology: Derived from 'Kleid' (garment/dress), from Middle High German 'kleit,' meaning 'clothing' as a collective term.
examples: Welche Kleidung soll ich mitnehmen? (What clothing should I bring?)
Die Kleidung in diesem Geschäft ist teuer. (The clothing in this store is expensive.)
details.usage: A collective noun typically used without a plural; Kleidungsstück means 'item of clothing' and Berufskleidung means 'work clothes.'

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'conto' (account), from Latin 'computus' (calculation), entering German through banking terminology.
examples: Ich möchte ein Konto eröffnen. (I would like to open an account.)
Das Geld ist auf meinem Konto. (The money is in my account.)
details.usage: The plural can also be Kontos in colloquial use; common compounds are Girokonto (checking account) and Sparkonto (savings account).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Kopf' (head) and 'Weh' (pain/ache), from Old High German 'wē,' a straightforward descriptive term for headache.
examples: Ich habe furchtbares Kopfweh. (I have a terrible headache.)
Von dem Lärm bekomme ich Kopfweh. (The noise gives me a headache.)
details.usage: Synonymous with Kopfschmerzen; Kopfweh is more colloquial and common in southern Germany, Austria, and Switzerland.

details.etymology: Derived from 'kreuzen' (to cross), from 'Kreuz' (cross), from Latin 'crux,' meaning 'a place where paths cross.'
examples: An der nächsten Kreuzung müssen Sie links abbiegen. (At the next intersection you have to turn left.)
Die Kreuzung ist sehr gefährlich. (The intersection is very dangerous.)
details.usage: Used for road intersections and also for biological crossbreeding; Straßenkreuzung specifically means 'road intersection.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'kündigen' (to terminate/give notice), from 'künden' (to announce), meaning 'termination' or 'notice.'
examples: Er hat seine Kündigung eingereicht. (He submitted his resignation.)
Die Kündigung des Mietvertrags muss schriftlich erfolgen. (The termination of the rental contract must be in writing.)
details.usage: Can refer to quitting a job, canceling a contract, or being fired; Kündigungsfrist means 'notice period.'

details.etymology: From Italian 'all'arme' (to arms!), a military alarm call that evolved to mean 'noise' or 'racket' in German.
examples: Die Nachbarn machen zu viel Lärm. (The neighbors are making too much noise.)
Wegen des Lärms konnte ich nicht schlafen. (Because of the noise I couldn't sleep.)
details.usage: Used without a plural; Lärmschutz means 'noise protection' and Lärmbelästigung means 'noise nuisance,' a common complaint in Germany.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ladan' (to load/invite), originally meaning a counter or display shelf, then evolving to mean 'shop.'
examples: Der Laden öffnet um neun Uhr. (The shop opens at nine o'clock.)
In der Altstadt gibt es viele kleine Läden. (In the old town there are many small shops.)
details.usage: More informal than Geschäft; also means 'shutter' in the compound Fensterladen (window shutter).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Land' (land) and the suffix '-schaft' (condition/state), meaning 'the character or appearance of a land area.'
examples: Die Landschaft in Bayern ist wunderschön. (The landscape in Bavaria is wonderful.)
Wir haben die Landschaft vom Zug aus bewundert. (We admired the landscape from the train.)
details.usage: Used in art (Landschaftsmalerei = landscape painting) and environmental contexts; Kulturlandschaft means 'cultural landscape.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Leben' (life) and 'Lauf' (course/run), literally meaning 'the course of one's life,' i.e., a CV or resume.
examples: Bitte schicken Sie Ihren Lebenslauf per E-Mail. (Please send your CV by email.)
Mein Lebenslauf ist zwei Seiten lang. (My CV is two pages long.)
details.usage: In Germany, a Lebenslauf traditionally includes a photo and personal details, unlike in many other countries.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'lioht,' related to Proto-Germanic *leuhtą and Latin 'lux,' meaning 'light' or 'illumination.'
examples: Kannst du bitte das Licht anmachen? (Can you please turn on the light?)
Am Abend sieht man die Lichter der Stadt. (In the evening you can see the city lights.)
details.usage: The plural Lichter refers to individual lights or candles; Tageslicht means 'daylight' and Ampellicht means 'traffic light.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'lust,' related to Proto-Germanic *lustuz, meaning 'desire,' 'pleasure,' or 'inclination.'
examples: Hast du Lust, ins Kino zu gehen? (Do you feel like going to the cinema?)
Ich habe keine Lust mehr. (I don't feel like it anymore.)
details.usage: The phrase 'Lust haben' (to feel like doing something) is extremely common in daily conversation and is not sexual in this context.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mago,' related to Proto-Germanic *magō, meaning 'stomach' or 'belly.'
examples: Mir tut der Magen weh. (My stomach hurts.)
Er hat Probleme mit dem Magen. (He has stomach problems.)
details.usage: Magenschmerzen means 'stomach pain' and Magengeschwür means 'stomach ulcer'; the phrase 'auf nüchternen Magen' means 'on an empty stomach.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Mann' (man) and '-schaft' (collective suffix), originally meaning 'a group of men,' now meaning 'team' or 'crew.'
examples: Unsere Mannschaft hat das Spiel gewonnen. (Our team won the game.)
Die Mannschaft hat hart trainiert. (The team trained hard.)
details.usage: Widely used in sports; the German national football team was traditionally called 'die Mannschaft'; Fußballmannschaft means 'football team.'

details.etymology: From Latin 'mercatus' (trade/market) via Old High German 'markat,' adopted during Roman trade contact.
examples: Am Samstag gehe ich auf den Markt. (On Saturday I go to the market.)
Der Wochenmarkt findet zweimal pro Woche statt. (The weekly market takes place twice a week.)
details.usage: Compounds include Supermarkt (supermarket), Weihnachtsmarkt (Christmas market), and Arbeitsmarkt (job market).

details.etymology: Derived from 'meinen' (to mean/think/opine), from Old High German 'meinōn,' meaning 'opinion.'
examples: Was ist deine Meinung dazu? (What is your opinion on that?)
Meiner Meinung nach ist das falsch. (In my opinion, that is wrong.)
details.usage: The phrase 'meiner Meinung nach' (in my opinion) is commonly abbreviated as 'm.M.n.' in writing.

details.etymology: From Latin 'missa' (Mass/church service), as medieval trade fairs were held during church festivals.
examples: Die Messe findet in Frankfurt statt. (The trade fair takes place in Frankfurt.)
Wir besuchen nächste Woche eine Messe. (We are visiting a trade fair next week.)
details.usage: Means both 'trade fair' and 'Catholic Mass'; Frankfurt and Hannover are Germany's most famous Messe cities.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mieta,' related to Proto-Germanic *mēdō (reward/payment), meaning 'rent' or 'rental payment.'
examples: Die Miete in München ist sehr hoch. (The rent in Munich is very high.)
Wir müssen die Miete am Ersten bezahlen. (We have to pay the rent on the first.)
details.usage: Mietvertrag means 'rental contract' and Mieter/Mieterin means 'tenant'; Germany has strong tenant protection laws (Mieterschutz).

details.etymology: Derived from 'möglich' (possible), from 'mögen' (to be able/may), meaning 'possibility' or 'option.'
examples: Es gibt viele Möglichkeiten. (There are many possibilities.)
Haben Sie die Möglichkeit, früher zu kommen? (Do you have the possibility to come earlier?)
details.usage: A very versatile word used in everyday speech; nach Möglichkeit means 'if possible.'

details.etymology: From Latin 'museum,' from Greek 'mouseion' (seat of the Muses), originally a place dedicated to the arts and learning.
examples: Das Museum hat montags geschlossen. (The museum is closed on Mondays.)
Wir besuchen morgen ein Museum. (We are visiting a museum tomorrow.)
details.usage: Note the unusual plural form Museen; Berlin's Museumsinsel (Museum Island) is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

details.etymology: From Latin 'natura' (birth/nature), from 'nasci' (to be born), borrowed into German in the Middle Ages.
examples: Ich bin gern in der Natur. (I like being in nature.)
Die Natur ist hier noch unberührt. (Nature is still untouched here.)
details.usage: The plural is rare; compounds include Naturschutz (nature conservation) and Naturwissenschaft (natural science).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'nebul,' related to Latin 'nebula' (cloud/mist) and Greek 'nephele,' meaning 'fog.'
examples: Heute Morgen war dichter Nebel. (This morning there was thick fog.)
Bei Nebel sollte man langsam fahren. (In fog, you should drive slowly.)
details.usage: The plural is identical to the singular; Nebelscheinwerfer means 'fog light' on a car.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Not' (need/distress) and 'Fall' (case), literally meaning 'a case of distress,' i.e., an emergency.
examples: Rufen Sie im Notfall die 112 an. (In an emergency, call 112.)
Das Krankenhaus hat eine Notaufnahme für Notfälle. (The hospital has an emergency room for emergencies.)
details.usage: 112 is the European emergency number; Notaufnahme means 'emergency room' and Notarzt means 'emergency doctor.'

details.etymology: From Latin 'numerus' (number) via French 'numéro,' entering German in the early modern period.
examples: Welche Nummer hat Ihr Zimmer? (What number is your room?)
Können Sie mir Ihre Telefonnummer geben? (Can you give me your phone number?)
details.usage: Used for phone numbers, house numbers, and identification numbers; compounds include Hausnummer and Kontonummer (account number).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ovan,' related to Proto-Germanic *uhnaz, meaning 'oven' or 'stove.'
examples: Der Kuchen ist im Ofen. (The cake is in the oven.)
Im Winter heizen wir mit dem Ofen. (In winter we heat with the stove.)
details.usage: Can mean 'oven' (Backofen), 'stove,' or 'furnace'; the Kachelofen (tiled stove) is a traditional feature in German homes.

details.etymology: From French 'paquet' (small package), diminutive form related to 'pack,' entering German in the 17th century.
examples: Ich erwarte ein Paket. (I am expecting a package.)
Können Sie dieses Paket für mich zur Post bringen? (Can you take this package to the post office for me?)
details.usage: Paketdienst means 'parcel service'; DHL, Hermes, and DPD are major package carriers in Germany.

details.etymology: Compound of 'parken' (to park, from English) and 'Platz' (place/square), a modern compound for 'parking spot.'
examples: Ich suche einen Parkplatz. (I am looking for a parking spot.)
Der Parkplatz ist voll. (The parking lot is full.)
details.usage: Parkhaus means 'parking garage' and Tiefgarage means 'underground parking'; finding a Parkplatz in German cities can be challenging.

details.etymology: From Latin 'pausa' (stop/rest) via French 'pause,' ultimately from Greek 'pausis' (cessation).
examples: Wir machen eine kurze Pause. (We are taking a short break.)
In der Pause essen die Kinder ihr Pausenbrot. (During the break, the children eat their snack.)
details.usage: Mittagspause (lunch break) is an important part of German work culture; Pausenbrot is a snack eaten during school break.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'pfliht,' meaning 'care' or 'obligation,' related to 'pflegen' (to care for).
examples: Es ist meine Pflicht, Ihnen das zu sagen. (It is my duty to tell you that.)
Jeder Bürger hat Rechte und Pflichten. (Every citizen has rights and duties.)
details.usage: Schulpflicht means 'compulsory education' and Versicherungspflicht means 'mandatory insurance'; Pflichtbewusst means 'dutiful.'

details.etymology: From Latin 'politia' (state administration) via French 'police,' from Greek 'politeia' (citizenship/government).
examples: Rufen Sie die Polizei! (Call the police!)
Die Polizei hat den Dieb gefangen. (The police caught the thief.)
details.usage: Usually used without a plural in everyday speech; the emergency number for police in Germany is 110; Polizeiwache means 'police station.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'prüfen' (to test/examine), from Latin 'probare' (to prove/test), meaning 'examination' or 'test.'
examples: Die Prüfung war sehr schwer. (The exam was very difficult.)
Wann ist deine nächste Prüfung? (When is your next exam?)
details.usage: Used for academic exams, driving tests, and inspections; Führerscheinprüfung is the driving test and Abschlussprüfung is the final exam.

details.etymology: From Latin 'proiectum' (something thrown forward) via French 'projet,' meaning 'a planned undertaking.'
examples: Wir arbeiten an einem neuen Projekt. (We are working on a new project.)
Das Projekt dauert drei Monate. (The project lasts three months.)
details.usage: Widely used in business and education; Projektleiter means 'project manager' and Projektarbeit means 'project work.'

details.etymology: From Latin 'qualitas' (quality/nature) via French 'qualité,' from 'qualis' (of what kind).
examples: Die Qualität dieses Produkts ist ausgezeichnet. (The quality of this product is excellent.)
Qualität hat ihren Preis. (Quality has its price.)
details.usage: Qualitätskontrolle means 'quality control'; 'Made in Germany' is associated with high Qualität worldwide.

details.etymology: From medieval Latin 'quietantia' (discharge/release) via French 'quittance,' meaning 'receipt' as proof of payment.
examples: Kann ich bitte eine Quittung haben? (Can I have a receipt, please?)
Bewahren Sie die Quittung gut auf. (Keep the receipt safe.)
details.usage: Different from Rechnung (invoice/bill): a Quittung is specifically the proof of payment received after paying.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Rat' (council/advice) and 'Haus' (house), literally 'council house,' where the city council meets.
examples: Das Rathaus steht am Marktplatz. (The town hall is on the market square.)
Wir haben im Rathaus geheiratet. (We got married at the town hall.)
details.usage: Every German city and town has a Rathaus; civil marriages (standesamtliche Trauung) take place there.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'rūm,' related to English 'room' and Proto-Germanic *rūmą, meaning 'space' or 'room.'
examples: Der Raum ist groß genug für zwanzig Personen. (The room is big enough for twenty people.)
Wir brauchen mehr Raum. (We need more space.)
details.usage: Means both 'room' and 'space'; compounds include Klassenzimmer is more specific than Klassenraum, while Weltraum means 'outer space.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'rīsa' (departure/journey), originally meaning 'a military expedition' before taking on the general sense of 'trip.'
examples: Gute Reise! (Have a good trip!)
Die Reise nach Italien war fantastisch. (The trip to Italy was fantastic.)
details.usage: Reisebüro means 'travel agency,' Reiseführer means 'travel guide,' and Dienstreise means 'business trip.'

details.etymology: From Latin 'reparatura' (restoration), from 'reparare' (to restore/repair), entering German through French.
examples: Die Reparatur kostet 200 Euro. (The repair costs 200 euros.)
Das Auto ist in der Werkstatt zur Reparatur. (The car is at the workshop for repair.)
details.usage: Reparaturwerkstatt means 'repair shop'; in Germany, there is a growing Reparaturkultur (repair culture) movement to reduce waste.

details.etymology: From Latin 'receptum' (received), originally a medical term meaning 'take this' at the start of a prescription.
examples: Der Arzt hat mir ein Rezept gegeben. (The doctor gave me a prescription.)
Hast du ein gutes Rezept für Kuchen? (Do you have a good recipe for cake?)
details.usage: Has two meanings: 'prescription' (medical) and 'recipe' (cooking); in Germany, many medicines require a Rezept from a doctor.

details.etymology: Derived from 'richten' (to direct/align), from Old High German 'rihten,' meaning 'direction.'
examples: In welche Richtung muss ich gehen? (In which direction do I have to go?)
Der Zug fährt in Richtung München. (The train is heading in the direction of Munich.)
details.usage: Commonly used with 'in Richtung' (in the direction of); also used figuratively for trends or orientations.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ruggi,' related to Proto-Germanic *hrugjaz, meaning 'back' (body part).
examples: Mir tut der Rücken weh. (My back hurts.)
Er hat Probleme mit dem Rücken. (He has back problems.)
details.usage: Rückenschmerzen (back pain) is one of the most common health complaints in Germany; Rucksack (backpack) is related.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ruowa,' related to Proto-Germanic *rōwō, meaning 'rest,' 'quiet,' or 'peace.'
examples: Ich brauche etwas Ruhe. (I need some peace and quiet.)
In Ruhe lassen heißt, jemanden nicht zu stören. (To leave in peace means not to disturb someone.)
details.usage: Ruhezeit (quiet hours) is taken seriously in Germany, especially between 22:00 and 6:00; Ruhestand means 'retirement.'

details.etymology: From Italian 'insalata' (salted), from Latin 'sal' (salt), originally referring to vegetables preserved with salt.
examples: Ich hätte gern einen gemischten Salat. (I would like a mixed salad.)
Kartoffelsalat ist typisch deutsch. (Potato salad is typically German.)
details.usage: Kartoffelsalat (potato salad) is a staple at German gatherings; also used colloquially in 'Da haben wir den Salat!' (What a mess!).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'scato,' related to Proto-Germanic *skadwaz, meaning 'shadow' or 'shade.'
examples: Lass uns im Schatten sitzen. (Let's sit in the shade.)
Der Baum wirft einen langen Schatten. (The tree casts a long shadow.)
details.usage: The plural is identical to the singular; Schattenseite means 'shady side' or figuratively 'downside.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sluzzil,' derived from 'sliozan' (to close/lock), meaning 'key' — the instrument for locking.
examples: Wo ist mein Schlüssel? (Where is my key?)
Ich habe meinen Schlüssel verloren. (I lost my key.)
details.usage: The plural is the same; compounds include Haustürschlüssel (house key), Schlüsselbund (keychain), and Schlüsselwort (keyword).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'smerzo,' related to Proto-Germanic *smertaz, meaning 'pain' both physical and emotional.
examples: Ich habe starke Schmerzen. (I have strong pain.)
Schmerzen im Rücken sind sehr häufig. (Back pain is very common.)
details.usage: Schmerzmittel means 'painkiller' and schmerzfrei means 'pain-free'; often used in the plural for physical pain.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'snupfe,' related to 'schnupfen' (to sniff), an onomatopoetic word describing nasal congestion.
examples: Ich habe einen Schnupfen. (I have a cold/runny nose.)
Bei Schnupfen hilft heißer Tee. (Hot tea helps with a cold.)
details.usage: Refers specifically to a runny/stuffy nose rather than a full cold (Erkältung); Heuschnupfen means 'hay fever.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'scranc' (barrier/fence), originally a barrier or enclosure, evolving to mean a closed storage piece.
examples: Die Kleider hängen im Schrank. (The clothes are hanging in the wardrobe.)
Wir brauchen einen neuen Schrank fürs Schlafzimmer. (We need a new wardrobe for the bedroom.)
details.usage: Compounds include Kleiderschrank (wardrobe), Kühlschrank (refrigerator), and Bücherschrank (bookcase).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sculd,' related to 'sollen' (should/ought), meaning both 'guilt/fault' and 'debt.'
examples: Es ist nicht meine Schuld. (It is not my fault.)
Er hat viele Schulden. (He has many debts.)
details.usage: In the singular, Schuld means 'guilt/fault'; in the plural, Schulden means 'debts' — schuldig means both 'guilty' and 'owing.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'schuz,' related to 'schützen' (to protect), meaning 'protection' or 'shelter.'
examples: Sonnencreme bietet Schutz vor der Sonne. (Sunscreen provides protection from the sun.)
Der Schutz der Umwelt ist sehr wichtig. (Environmental protection is very important.)
details.usage: Rarely used with a plural; Datenschutz means 'data protection,' Umweltschutz means 'environmental protection,' and Naturschutz means 'nature conservation.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'seifa,' a Germanic word borrowed into Latin as 'sapo,' originally a Germanic invention used as hair dye.
examples: Wasch dir die Hände mit Seife. (Wash your hands with soap.)
Diese Seife riecht sehr gut. (This soap smells very good.)
details.usage: Seifenoper means 'soap opera' (a calque from English); Seifenblase means 'soap bubble.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'senden' (to send), from Old High German 'senten,' meaning 'a shipment' or 'a broadcast program.'
examples: Die Sendung kommt um 20 Uhr. (The program airs at 8 PM.)
Meine Sendung ist noch nicht angekommen. (My shipment has not arrived yet.)
details.usage: Dual meaning: 'TV/radio program' (Fernsehsendung) and 'postal shipment' (Paketsendung).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sorga,' related to Proto-Germanic *surgō, meaning 'worry,' 'concern,' or 'care.'
examples: Mach dir keine Sorgen! (Don't worry!)
Die Eltern machen sich Sorgen um ihre Kinder. (The parents worry about their children.)
details.usage: The phrase 'sich Sorgen machen' (to worry) is extremely common; Fürsorge means 'welfare/care.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Speise' (food/dish) and 'Karte' (card/map), literally meaning 'food card,' i.e., a menu.
examples: Kann ich bitte die Speisekarte haben? (Can I have the menu, please?)
Auf der Speisekarte stehen viele vegetarische Gerichte. (There are many vegetarian dishes on the menu.)
details.usage: In more casual restaurants, Menü or Karte may be used instead; Getränkekarte is the drink menu.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sprāhha,' derived from 'sprechen' (to speak), meaning 'language' or 'speech.'
examples: Wie viele Sprachen sprichst du? (How many languages do you speak?)
Die deutsche Sprache hat viele zusammengesetzte Wörter. (The German language has many compound words.)
details.usage: Fremdsprache means 'foreign language,' Muttersprache means 'mother tongue,' and Sprachkurs means 'language course.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'stou,' related to 'stauen' (to dam up/block), meaning 'congestion' or 'traffic jam.'
examples: Auf der Autobahn ist Stau. (There is a traffic jam on the highway.)
Wegen des Staus kam ich zu spät. (Because of the traffic jam, I arrived late.)
details.usage: Staumeldung means 'traffic jam report'; German radio stations regularly broadcast Staumeldungen for the Autobahn.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stiura' (support/contribution), originally meaning 'a contribution to the community,' evolving to mean 'tax.'
examples: Die Steuern in Deutschland sind relativ hoch. (Taxes in Germany are relatively high.)
Muss ich dafür Steuer zahlen? (Do I have to pay tax on that?)
details.usage: Note: die Steuer (tax) vs. das Steuer (steering wheel) — same word, different gender and meaning; Steuererklärung is 'tax return.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stimma,' related to Proto-Germanic *stemnō, meaning 'voice' and also 'vote.'
examples: Sie hat eine schöne Stimme. (She has a beautiful voice.)
Bei der Wahl hat er viele Stimmen bekommen. (In the election, he received many votes.)
details.usage: Means both 'voice' and 'vote'; stimmen also means 'to be correct' (Das stimmt! = That's right!) and 'to tune' an instrument.

details.etymology: From Old French 'estoffe' (material/fabric), possibly from 'estoffer' (to furnish), entering German in the medieval period.
examples: Der Stoff für das Kleid ist aus Seide. (The fabric for the dress is silk.)
In der Schule lernen wir viel Stoff. (At school we learn a lot of material.)
details.usage: Means 'fabric/material,' 'substance' (Schadstoff = harmful substance), or 'subject matter' (Lernstoff = study material).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'strant,' related to Proto-Germanic *strandō, meaning 'shore' or 'beach.'
examples: Im Sommer gehen wir oft an den Strand. (In summer we often go to the beach.)
Der Strand war voller Menschen. (The beach was full of people.)
details.usage: Strandkorb (beach basket chair) is a uniquely German invention found on North Sea and Baltic coasts.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'strīt,' related to Proto-Germanic *strīdaz, meaning 'quarrel,' 'dispute,' or 'conflict.'
examples: Sie hatten einen Streit über Geld. (They had an argument about money.)
Der Streit zwischen den Nachbarn dauert schon lange. (The dispute between the neighbors has been going on for a long time.)
details.usage: The verb is 'streiten' (to argue); sich streiten means 'to quarrel'; Streitigkeit is the more formal term for 'dispute.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stucki,' meaning 'piece' or 'part,' related to Proto-Germanic *stukkją (a cut piece).
examples: Ich hätte gern ein Stück Kuchen. (I would like a piece of cake.)
Das Theaterstück war sehr gut. (The play was very good.)
details.usage: Extremely versatile: used as a counting word (drei Stück = three pieces), for theater (Theaterstück), and music (Musikstück).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'suppha,' related to 'saufen' (to drink) and French 'soupe,' originally meaning 'broth soaked with bread.'
examples: Die Suppe ist zu heiß. (The soup is too hot.)
Eine heiße Suppe tut im Winter gut. (A hot soup is good in winter.)
details.usage: The idiom 'Die Suppe auslöffeln, die man sich eingebrockt hat' means 'to face the consequences of one's actions.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'tasca,' possibly from medieval Latin 'tasca/tassia,' meaning 'bag' or 'pocket.'
examples: Meine Tasche ist sehr schwer. (My bag is very heavy.)
Er hat die Hände in den Taschen. (He has his hands in his pockets.)
details.usage: Means both 'bag' and 'pocket'; Handtasche is 'handbag,' Hosentasche is 'pants pocket,' and Taschengeld is 'pocket money.'

details.etymology: From Latin 'terminus' (boundary/limit), meaning 'a fixed date or appointment,' borrowed through legal and administrative language.
examples: Ich habe morgen einen Termin beim Arzt. (I have a doctor's appointment tomorrow.)
Können wir einen Termin vereinbaren? (Can we schedule an appointment?)
details.usage: Essential in German daily life; einen Termin machen/vereinbaren means 'to make an appointment'; Terminkalender is an appointment calendar.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'topf,' related to Proto-Germanic *duppaz, meaning 'pot' or 'saucepan.'
examples: Das Wasser kocht im Topf. (The water is boiling in the pot.)
Stell den Topf auf den Herd. (Put the pot on the stove.)
details.usage: Blumentopf means 'flower pot'; the idiom 'damit kann man keinen Blumentopf gewinnen' means 'that won't get you anywhere.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'treppa,' related to 'treten' (to step/tread), meaning 'stairs' or 'staircase.'
examples: Die Treppe führt in den zweiten Stock. (The stairs lead to the second floor.)
Vorsicht auf der Treppe! (Be careful on the stairs!)
details.usage: Treppenhaus means 'stairwell'; Rolltreppe means 'escalator' (literally 'rolling stairs').

details.etymology: Derived from 'überraschen' (to surprise), compound of 'über' (over) and 'raschen' (to rush), meaning 'to rush over someone unexpectedly.'
examples: Was für eine schöne Überraschung! (What a lovely surprise!)
Die Party war eine Überraschung für sie. (The party was a surprise for her.)
details.usage: Überraschungsei (surprise egg, like Kinder Surprise) is a famous German product; Überraschungsparty means 'surprise party.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'umgeben' (to surround), compound of 'um' (around) and 'geben' (to give), meaning 'surroundings.'
examples: Die Umgebung der Stadt ist sehr grün. (The surroundings of the city are very green.)
In der Umgebung gibt es viele Wanderwege. (In the surrounding area there are many hiking trails.)
details.usage: Often used to describe the area around a city or location; in IT, Umgebung can also mean 'environment' (Entwicklungsumgebung = development environment).

details.etymology: Compound of 'um' (around/re-) and 'Zug' (pull/move), meaning 'a move' (changing residence) or 'a procession/parade.'
examples: Unser Umzug ist nächste Woche. (Our move is next week.)
Der Karnevalsumzug war sehr bunt. (The carnival parade was very colorful.)
details.usage: Has two meanings: moving to a new home (umziehen) and a procession/parade; Umzugskarton means 'moving box.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'unterrichten' (to teach/instruct), compound of 'unter' (under) and 'richten' (to direct), meaning 'instruction.'
examples: Der Unterricht beginnt um acht Uhr. (Classes begin at eight o'clock.)
Heute fällt der Unterricht aus. (Today classes are canceled.)
details.usage: Rarely has a plural; Deutschunterricht means 'German class,' Musikunterricht means 'music lessons.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'unter' (under/between) and 'Schied' (from 'scheiden,' to separate), meaning 'the thing that separates' — a difference.
examples: Was ist der Unterschied zwischen A und B? (What is the difference between A and B?)
Es gibt große Unterschiede zwischen den beiden Produkten. (There are big differences between the two products.)
details.usage: The verb 'unterscheiden' means 'to distinguish'; unterschiedlich means 'different/various.'

details.etymology: From 'vereinen' (to unite), derived from 'ein' (one), meaning 'a union' or 'club/association.'
examples: Er ist Mitglied in einem Sportverein. (He is a member of a sports club.)
In Deutschland gibt es über 600.000 Vereine. (In Germany there are over 600,000 clubs.)
details.usage: Germany's Vereinskultur (club culture) is famous; almost every activity has a Verein, and 'eingetragener Verein' (e.V.) is a registered association.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'verkēr,' derived from 'verkehren' (to turn around/to frequent), meaning 'traffic' or 'transportation.'
examples: Im Verkehr muss man vorsichtig sein. (In traffic, you have to be careful.)
Der Verkehr in der Stadt ist sehr dicht. (The traffic in the city is very heavy.)
details.usage: Rarely used in plural; Verkehrsmittel means 'means of transport,' Verkehrsregel means 'traffic rule,' and Berufsverkehr means 'rush hour traffic.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'vertrac,' derived from 'vertragen' (to endure/agree), meaning 'contract' or 'agreement.'
examples: Wir haben den Vertrag unterschrieben. (We signed the contract.)
Der Vertrag läuft zwei Jahre. (The contract runs for two years.)
details.usage: Mietvertrag (rental contract), Arbeitsvertrag (employment contract), and Handyvertrag (phone contract) are common types.

details.etymology: Derived from 'verspäten' (to be late), from 'spät' (late), meaning 'delay' or 'lateness.'
examples: Der Zug hat zehn Minuten Verspätung. (The train is ten minutes late.)
Entschuldigung für die Verspätung. (Sorry for the delay.)
details.usage: Despite Germany's reputation for punctuality, Deutsche Bahn Verspätungen are a frequent topic of complaint and humor.

details.etymology: Compound of 'vor' (before/forward) and 'Schlag' (hit/stroke), meaning 'something put forward' — a suggestion or proposal.
examples: Ich habe einen Vorschlag. (I have a suggestion.)
Dein Vorschlag klingt gut. (Your suggestion sounds good.)
details.usage: The verb is 'vorschlagen' (to suggest); Verbesserungsvorschlag means 'improvement suggestion.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'vor' (before/ahead) and 'Teil' (part/share), originally meaning 'the part one gets first' — an advantage.
examples: Das hat viele Vorteile. (That has many advantages.)
Der Vorteil dieser Methode ist die Einfachheit. (The advantage of this method is its simplicity.)
details.usage: The opposite is Nachteil (disadvantage); vorteilhaft means 'advantageous.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'wahr' (true), from Old High German 'wār,' with the abstract suffix '-heit,' meaning 'the state of being true.'
examples: Sag mir die Wahrheit! (Tell me the truth!)
Die Wahrheit ist manchmal schwer zu akzeptieren. (The truth is sometimes hard to accept.)
details.usage: The phrase 'um die Wahrheit zu sagen' means 'to tell the truth'; 'Wahrheit oder Pflicht' is the game 'Truth or Dare.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'wandern' (to hike/wander), from Old High German 'wantarōn,' meaning 'a hike' or 'trek.'
examples: Wir machen am Sonntag eine Wanderung. (We are going on a hike on Sunday.)
Die Wanderung dauert etwa drei Stunden. (The hike takes about three hours.)
details.usage: Wandern (hiking) is one of Germany's most popular pastimes; Wanderweg means 'hiking trail' and Wanderschuhe means 'hiking boots.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Werk' (work) and 'Statt' (place), literally meaning 'workplace,' from Old High German.
examples: Das Auto ist in der Werkstatt. (The car is at the repair shop.)
Er arbeitet in einer Werkstatt für Möbel. (He works in a furniture workshop.)
details.usage: Autowerkstatt means 'car repair shop'; also used for creative spaces (Schreibwerkstatt = writing workshop).

details.etymology: Derived from 'werben' (to advertise/recruit), from Old High German 'werban' (to turn/strive), meaning 'advertising.'
examples: Im Fernsehen kommt zu viel Werbung. (There is too much advertising on TV.)
Die Werbung für das neue Produkt ist überall. (The advertising for the new product is everywhere.)
details.usage: The plural is uncommon; Werbung is usually collective; Werbespot means 'commercial' and Werbefläche means 'advertising space.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wetar,' related to Proto-Germanic *wedrą and English 'weather,' meaning 'atmospheric conditions.'
examples: Wie ist das Wetter heute? (How is the weather today?)
Das Wetter soll morgen besser werden. (The weather is supposed to get better tomorrow.)
details.usage: Wettervorhersage means 'weather forecast'; talking about the Wetter is a universal conversation starter in Germany too.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wunsc,' related to Proto-Germanic *wunskaz, meaning 'wish' or 'desire.'
examples: Haben Sie noch einen Wunsch? (Do you have another wish?)
Mein größter Wunsch ist eine Reise nach Japan. (My biggest wish is a trip to Japan.)
details.usage: Wunschliste means 'wish list'; auf Wunsch means 'upon request'; 'nach Wunsch' means 'as desired.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Zahn' (tooth) and 'Arzt' (doctor), literally 'tooth doctor,' a straightforward German compound.
examples: Ich muss zum Zahnarzt gehen. (I have to go to the dentist.)
Der Zahnarzt hat mir einen Zahn gezogen. (The dentist pulled one of my teeth.)
details.usage: Zahnarztpraxis means 'dental practice'; the feminine form is Zahnärztin; regular dental checkups are covered by German health insurance.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'giziugnis,' derived from 'Zeuge' (witness), originally meaning 'testimony' and later 'certificate/report card.'
examples: Mein Zeugnis war dieses Jahr sehr gut. (My report card was very good this year.)
Für die Bewerbung brauche ich mein Arbeitszeugnis. (For the application I need my work reference.)
details.usage: Schulzeugnis is a school report card and Arbeitszeugnis is a work reference letter — both are very important documents in Germany.

details.etymology: Compound of 'zu' (to/toward) and 'Kunft' (coming, from 'kommen'), literally meaning 'what is coming toward us' — the future.
examples: Was sind deine Pläne für die Zukunft? (What are your plans for the future?)
In Zukunft werde ich pünktlicher sein. (In the future I will be more punctual.)
details.usage: The plural is rare; zukünftig means 'future/forthcoming'; Zukunftsangst means 'fear of the future.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zil,' related to Proto-Germanic *tīlą, meaning 'target,' 'goal,' or 'destination.'
examples: Mein Ziel ist es, Deutsch zu lernen. (My goal is to learn German.)
Was ist das Ziel unserer Reise? (What is the destination of our trip?)
details.usage: Means both 'goal/aim' and 'destination'; Reiseziel means 'travel destination' and Ziellinie means 'finish line.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Abend' (evening) and 'Essen' (food/meal), literally 'evening meal,' a straightforward descriptive compound.
examples: Was gibt es zum Abendessen? (What is there for dinner?)
Das Abendessen ist um sieben Uhr. (Dinner is at seven o'clock.)
details.usage: The plural is identical to the singular; a traditional German Abendessen (Abendbrot) often consists of bread, cold cuts, and cheese.

details.etymology: Derived from 'abteilen' (to divide off/section), compound of 'ab' (off) and 'teilen' (to divide), meaning 'department' or 'section.'
examples: In welcher Abteilung arbeiten Sie? (In which department do you work?)
Die Abteilung für Kleidung ist im zweiten Stock. (The clothing department is on the second floor.)
details.usage: Used both in companies (Personalabteilung = HR department) and in stores (departments/sections).

details.etymology: From Latin 'apotheca' (storehouse), from Greek 'apotheke' (storeroom), originally a place for storing goods, later specialized to medicines.
examples: Ich muss zur Apotheke gehen. (I have to go to the pharmacy.)
Die Apotheke hat auch am Sonntag Notdienst. (The pharmacy also has emergency service on Sundays.)
details.usage: German Apotheken are strictly regulated and distinguished from Drogerien (drugstores that sell non-prescription items); they display a red 'A' sign.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Arbeit' (work) and 'Platz' (place), literally meaning 'work place,' referring to both a job and a physical workspace.
examples: Mein Arbeitsplatz ist sehr modern. (My workspace is very modern.)
Die Firma schafft neue Arbeitsplätze. (The company is creating new jobs.)
details.usage: Can mean both 'workplace/desk' and 'job position'; Arbeitsplatzwechsel means 'job change.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'auf' (up/on) and 'Gabe' (gift/giving), originally meaning 'something given up' and later 'task' or 'assignment.'
examples: Das ist eine schwierige Aufgabe. (That is a difficult task.)
Hast du deine Aufgaben schon gemacht? (Have you already done your assignments?)
details.usage: Used for tasks, homework (Hausaufgabe), and duties; can also mean 'giving up' (die Aufgabe eines Plans = abandoning a plan).

details.etymology: Compound of 'aus' (out) and 'Land' (land/country), literally 'out-land,' meaning 'foreign countries' collectively.
examples: Er lebt im Ausland. (He lives abroad.)
Waren Sie schon mal im Ausland? (Have you ever been abroad?)
details.usage: Used without a plural; Ausländer means 'foreigner,' ins Ausland means 'to go abroad,' and Auslandsaufenthalt means 'stay abroad.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'aus' (out) and 'Weis' (from 'weisen,' to show/prove), meaning 'something that shows/proves identity.'
examples: Zeigen Sie bitte Ihren Ausweis. (Please show your ID.)
Mein Ausweis ist abgelaufen. (My ID has expired.)
details.usage: Personalausweis is the German national identity card; Reisepass is the passport; carrying ID is mandatory in Germany.

details.etymology: Derived from 'Bäcker' (baker) with the suffix '-ei' indicating a place of business, from 'backen' (to bake).
examples: Ich kaufe Brötchen in der Bäckerei. (I buy rolls at the bakery.)
Die Bäckerei öffnet schon um fünf Uhr morgens. (The bakery opens as early as five in the morning.)
details.usage: German Bäckereien are a cornerstone of daily life, offering bread, Brötchen, and pastries; Germany has over 300 types of bread.

details.etymology: From Italian 'balcone' (large window/platform), from Old Italian 'balco' (scaffold), of Germanic origin related to 'Balken' (beam).
examples: Wir frühstücken auf dem Balkon. (We have breakfast on the balcony.)
Der Balkon hat einen schönen Blick auf den Garten. (The balcony has a lovely view of the garden.)
details.usage: Also spelled Balkons in plural colloquially; pronunciation varies regionally (stress on first or second syllable).

details.etymology: From French 'batterie' (a beating/battery), from 'battre' (to beat), originally a military term for a group of cannons.
examples: Die Batterie ist leer. (The battery is dead.)
Wir brauchen neue Batterien für die Fernbedienung. (We need new batteries for the remote control.)
details.usage: In Germany, used batteries must be returned to collection points (Sammelstellen) for recycling by law.

details.etymology: Derived from 'bedienen' (to serve/operate), compound of 'be-' (prefix) and 'dienen' (to serve), meaning 'service' or 'operation.'
examples: Die Bedienung in diesem Restaurant ist sehr gut. (The service in this restaurant is very good.)
Die Bedienung des Geräts ist einfach. (The operation of the device is simple.)
details.usage: Means both 'service/waiter' and 'operation of a device'; Bedienungsanleitung means 'operating manual.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'bestellen' (to order), compound of 'be-' and 'stellen' (to place), meaning 'an order placed.'
examples: Ihre Bestellung ist unterwegs. (Your order is on its way.)
Ich möchte eine Bestellung aufgeben. (I would like to place an order.)
details.usage: Used for restaurant orders, online shopping, and deliveries; eine Bestellung aufgeben means 'to place an order.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'bewerben' (to apply), compound of 'be-' and 'werben' (to advertise/recruit), meaning 'application.'
examples: Ich habe meine Bewerbung abgeschickt. (I sent my application.)
Für die Bewerbung brauche ich einen Lebenslauf. (For the application I need a CV.)
details.usage: A German Bewerbung typically includes an Anschreiben (cover letter), Lebenslauf (CV), and Zeugnisse (certificates).

details.etymology: From Latin 'bibliotheca,' from Greek 'bibliotheke' (book repository), compound of 'biblion' (book) and 'theke' (storage place).
examples: Ich leihe mir Bücher aus der Bibliothek. (I borrow books from the library.)
Die Bibliothek hat bis 22 Uhr geöffnet. (The library is open until 10 PM.)
details.usage: Stadtbibliothek means 'city library' and Universitätsbibliothek means 'university library'; the informal short form is Bibo.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Bild' (picture/image) and 'Schirm' (screen/shield), literally meaning 'picture screen.'
examples: Der Bildschirm ist zu klein. (The screen is too small.)
Schau nicht so lange auf den Bildschirm. (Don't look at the screen for so long.)
details.usage: Used for computer monitors, TV screens, and any display; Touchscreen is also used in German as a loanword.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'blat,' related to Proto-Germanic *bladą, meaning both 'leaf' of a plant and 'sheet' of paper.
examples: Im Herbst fallen die Blätter von den Bäumen. (In autumn the leaves fall from the trees.)
Gib mir bitte ein Blatt Papier. (Please give me a sheet of paper.)
details.usage: Dual meaning: 'leaf' (botanical) and 'sheet' (paper); also means a 'hand' of cards (ein gutes Blatt haben).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Brief' (letter) and 'Kasten' (box), literally 'letter box,' a straightforward German compound.
examples: Der Briefkasten ist an der Haustür. (The mailbox is at the front door.)
Hast du schon in den Briefkasten geschaut? (Have you already checked the mailbox?)
details.usage: Yellow Briefkästen on the street are for sending mail (Deutsche Post); residential ones are for receiving mail.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'chance' (luck/opportunity), from Latin 'cadentia' (falling, as in dice falling), entering German in the 17th century.
examples: Das ist deine Chance! (This is your chance!)
Die Chancen stehen gut. (The chances are good.)
details.usage: Chancengleichheit means 'equal opportunity'; often used in sports commentary for scoring opportunities.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'dah,' related to Proto-Germanic *þaką (covering), meaning 'roof' — that which covers a building.
examples: Das Dach muss repariert werden. (The roof needs to be repaired.)
Unter einem Dach wohnen viele Familien. (Many families live under one roof.)
details.usage: Dachgeschoss means 'attic floor'; Dachboden means 'attic'; the idiom 'unter Dach und Fach' means 'settled/finalized.'

details.etymology: From Latin 'diaeta,' from Greek 'diaita' (way of life/diet), originally meaning a prescribed way of living.
