examples: Sie macht gerade eine Diät. (She is currently on a diet.)
Der Arzt hat ihm eine Diät empfohlen. (The doctor recommended a diet for him.)
details.usage: In political context, Diäten (plural) also means the salary of members of parliament.

details.etymology: Shortened from 'Diskothek,' from French 'discothèque,' modeled on 'bibliothèque,' meaning 'a place for discs/records.'
examples: Am Samstag gehen wir in die Disko. (On Saturday we are going to the disco.)
Die Disko war sehr voll. (The disco was very crowded.)
details.usage: Also written as Disco; somewhat dated — younger Germans more commonly say Club or Klub.

details.etymology: From Latin 'documentum' (lesson/proof), from 'docere' (to teach), meaning 'a written record that proves or documents something.'
examples: Bitte bringen Sie alle Dokumente mit. (Please bring all documents with you.)
Das Dokument muss unterschrieben werden. (The document must be signed.)
details.usage: Dokumentation means 'documentation'; in IT, Dokument also refers to digital files.

details.etymology: From Middle Low German 'dose,' possibly from medieval Latin 'dosis' (a container for a measured amount), meaning 'can' or 'tin.'
examples: Kaufst du bitte eine Dose Tomaten? (Can you buy a can of tomatoes, please?)
Die Dose ist aus Aluminium. (The can is made of aluminum.)
details.usage: Getränkedose means 'beverage can'; Germany has a Dosenpfand (can deposit) system requiring deposits on disposable cans.

details.etymology: From French 'douche,' from Italian 'doccia' (water pipe), originally from Latin 'ductio' (a leading/conducting of water).
examples: Ich nehme morgens eine Dusche. (I take a shower in the morning.)
Die Dusche funktioniert nicht. (The shower is not working.)
details.usage: Duschen is also the verb form (to shower); Duschgel means 'shower gel' and Duschvorhang means 'shower curtain.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'ein' (in) and 'Kauf' (purchase), literally meaning 'buying in' — i.e., shopping or a purchase.
examples: Ich muss noch Einkäufe machen. (I still have to do some shopping.)
Der Einkauf im Supermarkt war teuer. (The shopping at the supermarket was expensive.)
details.usage: Einkaufen gehen means 'to go shopping'; Einkaufszentrum means 'shopping center' and Einkaufsliste means 'shopping list.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'eninchilī,' a diminutive of 'ano' (grandfather), originally meaning 'little grandfather' — i.e., the one who resembles the grandfather.
examples: Sie hat drei Enkel. (She has three grandchildren.)
Der Enkel besucht seine Großeltern am Wochenende. (The grandson visits his grandparents on the weekend.)
details.usage: The feminine form is Enkelin (granddaughter); Enkelkind means 'grandchild' and is gender-neutral.

details.etymology: Derived from 'entfernen' (to remove/distance), compound of 'ent-' (away) and 'fernen' (from 'fern,' far), meaning 'distance.'
examples: Die Entfernung zwischen Berlin und München beträgt 600 Kilometer. (The distance between Berlin and Munich is 600 kilometers.)
Aus der Entfernung sieht es anders aus. (From a distance it looks different.)
details.usage: Entfernung means 'distance' or 'removal'; in der Entfernung means 'in the distance.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'ergeben' (to result/yield), compound of 'er-' and 'geben' (to give), meaning 'that which is yielded' — a result.
examples: Das Ergebnis der Prüfung war gut. (The result of the exam was good.)
Wir warten auf die Ergebnisse. (We are waiting for the results.)
details.usage: Widely used in academic, sports, and business contexts; Suchergebnis means 'search result' and Wahlergebnis means 'election result.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'erkälten' (to catch cold), from 'kalt' (cold), reflecting the old belief that cold weather causes colds.
examples: Ich habe eine Erkältung. (I have a cold.)
Bei einer Erkältung sollte man viel trinken. (When you have a cold, you should drink a lot.)
details.usage: Refers to a common cold; more severe than Schnupfen but less serious than Grippe (flu); sich erkälten means 'to catch a cold.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'ermäßigen' (to reduce), from 'mäßig' (moderate), meaning 'a reduction' or 'discount.'
examples: Gibt es eine Ermäßigung für Studenten? (Is there a discount for students?)
Mit dieser Karte bekommen Sie eine Ermäßigung von 20%. (With this card you get a 20% discount.)
details.usage: Common at museums, theaters, and public transport; Studentenermäßigung means 'student discount.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fah,' meaning 'division' or 'compartment,' related to 'fangen' (to catch/contain).
examples: Mathematik ist mein Lieblingsfach. (Mathematics is my favorite subject.)
Leg das Buch ins Fach. (Put the book in the compartment.)
details.usage: Means both 'subject' (school) and 'compartment/shelf'; Fachmann means 'expert' and Schulfach means 'school subject.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Feier' (celebration) and 'Abend' (evening), literally 'celebration evening,' originally the evening rest after a day's work.
examples: Um 17 Uhr ist Feierabend. (Work ends at 5 PM.)
Was machst du nach Feierabend? (What do you do after work?)
details.usage: A uniquely German concept with no direct English equivalent; Feierabend marks the boundary between work and personal time, deeply respected in German culture.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Feuer' (fire) and 'Wehr' (defense), literally 'fire defense,' meaning 'fire brigade.'
examples: Ruf die Feuerwehr! (Call the fire department!)
Die Feuerwehr war schnell vor Ort. (The fire department arrived quickly on the scene.)
details.usage: The German fire department number is 112; Freiwillige Feuerwehr (volunteer fire brigade) plays a major role in smaller communities.

details.etymology: From Italian 'firma' (signature/confirmation), from Latin 'firmare' (to confirm), originally the confirmed name of a business.
examples: Die Firma hat 200 Mitarbeiter. (The company has 200 employees.)
Für welche Firma arbeiten Sie? (Which company do you work for?)
details.usage: Note the irregular plural Firmen; GmbH (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung) is the most common company form in Germany.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fluor,' meaning 'floor' or 'open land,' later specialized to mean 'hallway' or 'corridor.'
examples: Häng deinen Mantel im Flur auf. (Hang your coat in the hallway.)
Der Flur ist lang und dunkel. (The hallway is long and dark.)
details.usage: Means 'hallway/corridor' (masculine: der Flur); note that 'die Flur' (feminine) means 'open field' — different gender, different meaning.

details.etymology: From the Föhn wind in the Alps (Latin 'favonius,' a warm west wind), later applied to hair dryers that blow warm air.
examples: Leihst du mir deinen Föhn? (Will you lend me your hair dryer?)
Der Föhn ist im Bad. (The hair dryer is in the bathroom.)
details.usage: Also refers to the warm Alpine wind (Föhnwind); Föhn is a genericized trademark in German, similar to 'Kleenex' in English.

details.etymology: Compound of 'frei' (free/open-air) and 'Bad' (bath/pool), meaning 'open-air swimming pool.'
examples: Im Sommer gehen wir oft ins Freibad. (In summer we often go to the outdoor pool.)
Das Freibad öffnet im Mai. (The outdoor pool opens in May.)
details.usage: Freibäder are popular in Germany during summer; the indoor counterpart is Hallenbad; Schwimmbad is the general term.

details.etymology: Compound of 'frei' (free) and 'Zeit' (time), literally 'free time,' meaning 'leisure time.'
examples: Was machst du in deiner Freizeit? (What do you do in your free time?)
Ich habe leider wenig Freizeit. (Unfortunately, I have little free time.)
details.usage: Rarely has a plural; Freizeitaktivität means 'leisure activity' and Freizeitpark means 'amusement park.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Freund' (friend) and '-schaft' (collective/state suffix), from Old High German 'friuntscaf,' meaning 'friendship.'
examples: Unsere Freundschaft ist mir sehr wichtig. (Our friendship is very important to me.)
Wahre Freundschaft hält ein Leben lang. (True friendship lasts a lifetime.)
details.usage: Freundschaftsspiel means 'friendly match' in sports; Germans tend to distinguish clearly between Freundschaft and Bekanntschaft (acquaintanceship).

details.etymology: From French 'friseur' (hairdresser), from 'friser' (to curl), entering German in the 18th century.
examples: Ich muss zum Friseur gehen. (I have to go to the hairdresser.)
Der Friseur hat mir die Haare zu kurz geschnitten. (The hairdresser cut my hair too short.)
details.usage: The feminine form is Friseurin or Frisörin; also spelled Frisör; Friseursalon means 'hair salon.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gebūwide,' derived from 'bauen' (to build) with the collective prefix 'ge-,' meaning 'a built structure.'
examples: Das Gebäude ist sehr alt. (The building is very old.)
In diesem Gebäude sind viele Büros. (There are many offices in this building.)
details.usage: The plural is identical to the singular; Hochhaus (high-rise) and Wohngebäude (residential building) are common types.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gehalt,' derived from 'halten' (to hold/contain), meaning 'that which is held/contained' — i.e., salary.
examples: Sein Gehalt wird am Ende des Monats überwiesen. (His salary is transferred at the end of the month.)
Sie verdient ein gutes Gehalt. (She earns a good salary.)
details.usage: Das Gehalt means 'salary' (for white-collar workers); der Gehalt means 'content/substance' — different gender, different meaning.

details.etymology: Derived from 'genehmigen' (to approve/permit), from 'genehm' (agreeable), meaning 'approval' or 'permit.'
examples: Sie brauchen eine Genehmigung für den Umbau. (You need a permit for the renovation.)
Die Genehmigung wurde erteilt. (The permit was granted.)
details.usage: Baugenehmigung means 'building permit'; German bureaucracy often requires various Genehmigungen for many activities.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gerāte,' derived from 'Rat' (counsel/equipment) with the prefix 'ge-,' meaning 'device' or 'appliance.'
examples: Dieses Gerät ist sehr praktisch. (This device is very practical.)
Schalten Sie alle elektronischen Geräte aus. (Turn off all electronic devices.)
details.usage: Haushaltsgerät means 'household appliance,' Sportgerät means 'sports equipment,' and Elektrogerät means 'electrical device.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'girihti,' derived from 'richten' (to judge/direct), meaning both 'court of law' and 'dish' (prepared food).
examples: Das Gericht hat ihn freigesprochen. (The court acquitted him.)
Dieses Gericht schmeckt ausgezeichnet. (This dish tastes excellent.)
details.usage: Dual meaning: 'court' (legal) and 'dish' (food); Hauptgericht means 'main course' and Amtsgericht means 'district court.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gesmac,' related to 'schmecken' (to taste), with the prefix 'ge-,' meaning 'taste' or 'flavor.'
examples: Der Kuchen hat einen guten Geschmack. (The cake has a good taste.)
Über Geschmack lässt sich nicht streiten. (There is no accounting for taste.)
details.usage: The plural is rare; the proverb 'Über Geschmack lässt sich nicht streiten' is the German equivalent of 'to each their own'; geschmackvoll means 'tasteful.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gewiht,' derived from 'wegen' (to weigh) with the prefix 'ge-,' meaning 'weight.'
examples: Das Gewicht des Koffers darf 23 Kilo nicht überschreiten. (The weight of the suitcase must not exceed 23 kilos.)
Er hebt schwere Gewichte. (He lifts heavy weights.)
details.usage: Übergewicht means 'overweight' or 'excess weight'; Körpergewicht means 'body weight.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'giwin,' derived from 'gewinnen' (to win/gain), meaning 'profit,' 'gain,' or 'prize.'
examples: Die Firma hat dieses Jahr einen großen Gewinn gemacht. (The company made a big profit this year.)
Herzlichen Glückwunsch zum Gewinn! (Congratulations on the win!)
details.usage: Means both 'profit' (financial) and 'winnings/prize'; Gewinnspiel means 'competition/sweepstakes.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'gewohnt' (accustomed), from 'wohnen' (to dwell), with '-heit' suffix, meaning 'habit' or 'custom.'
examples: Das ist eine schlechte Gewohnheit. (That is a bad habit.)
Aus Gewohnheit stehe ich früh auf. (Out of habit I get up early.)
details.usage: The phrase 'aus Gewohnheit' means 'out of habit'; Gewohnheitstier means someone who is a creature of habit.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gras,' related to Proto-Germanic *grasą and English 'grass,' meaning 'grass.'
examples: Die Kinder spielen auf dem Gras. (The children are playing on the grass.)
Das Gras muss gemäht werden. (The grass needs to be mowed.)
details.usage: The idiom 'ins Gras beißen' (to bite the grass) means 'to die' (colloquial); 'Gras über etwas wachsen lassen' means 'to let things blow over.'

details.etymology: From Old High German 'grif,' derived from 'greifen' (to grasp/grab), meaning 'handle,' 'grip,' or 'grasp.'
examples: Der Griff an der Tür ist kaputt. (The handle on the door is broken.)
Er hat die Situation im Griff. (He has the situation under control.)
details.usage: The phrase 'etwas im Griff haben' means 'to have something under control'; Handgriff means 'handle' or 'manual action.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Grund' (ground/land) and 'Stück' (piece), literally meaning 'a piece of land' — a plot or property.
examples: Das Grundstück ist 500 Quadratmeter groß. (The property is 500 square meters.)
Sie haben ein Grundstück am See gekauft. (They bought a property by the lake.)
details.usage: Grundstückspreis means 'land price'; in Germany, buying a Grundstück requires a Notar (notary) for the transaction.

details.etymology: Compound of 'gut' (good) and 'Schein' (certificate/note), literally 'good note' — a voucher or gift card.
examples: Ich habe einen Gutschein für das Restaurant. (I have a voucher for the restaurant.)
Zum Geburtstag hat sie einen Gutschein bekommen. (For her birthday she received a gift card.)
details.usage: Geschenkgutschein means 'gift voucher'; in Germany, Gutscheine are valid for at least 3 years by law.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'halbida,' derived from 'halb' (half), meaning 'one half' of something.
examples: Die Hälfte der Klasse war krank. (Half of the class was sick.)
Wir teilen die Pizza in zwei Hälften. (We divide the pizza into two halves.)
details.usage: Used with genitive or von: die Hälfte der Zeit (half the time); erste Hälfte / zweite Hälfte are used in sports for first/second half.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Hand' (hand) and 'Werk' (work), literally 'hand work,' meaning 'craft' or 'trade.'
examples: Er hat ein Handwerk gelernt. (He learned a trade.)
Handwerk hat goldenen Boden. (A trade provides a solid livelihood.)
details.usage: The proverb 'Handwerk hat goldenen Boden' emphasizes the value of skilled trades; Handwerker means 'craftsman.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Haupt' (head/main) and 'Stadt' (city), literally 'head city' — the capital city.
examples: Berlin ist die Hauptstadt von Deutschland. (Berlin is the capital of Germany.)
Welche Hauptstadt hat die meisten Einwohner? (Which capital has the most inhabitants?)
details.usage: Each German Bundesland (state) also has its own Landeshauptstadt (state capital).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Haus' (house) and 'Arbeit' (work), meaning both 'housework' and 'academic paper written at home.'
examples: Die Hausarbeit ist bis nächste Woche fällig. (The term paper is due by next week.)
Er hilft nie bei der Hausarbeit. (He never helps with the housework.)
details.usage: Dual meaning: 'housework/chores' and 'academic term paper'; context determines which is meant.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Haus' (house) and 'Aufgabe' (task), literally 'house task' — homework assigned by school.
examples: Hast du deine Hausaufgaben gemacht? (Have you done your homework?)
Die Lehrerin hat viele Hausaufgaben aufgegeben. (The teacher assigned a lot of homework.)
details.usage: Almost always used in the plural; also used figuratively: 'seine Hausaufgaben machen' means 'to do one's due diligence.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'hoch' (high) and 'Zeit' (time), originally meaning 'high time' or 'festive occasion,' specialized to mean 'wedding.'
examples: Die Hochzeit findet im Juni statt. (The wedding takes place in June.)
Wir sind zur Hochzeit eingeladen. (We are invited to the wedding.)
details.usage: Standesamtliche Hochzeit is a civil wedding (legally required in Germany); kirchliche Hochzeit is a church wedding (optional).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hof,' related to Proto-Germanic *hufą, meaning 'yard,' 'courtyard,' or 'farm.'
examples: Die Kinder spielen im Hof. (The children are playing in the yard.)
Der Bauernhof liegt auf dem Land. (The farm is in the countryside.)
details.usage: Bauernhof means 'farm,' Innenhof means 'inner courtyard,' and Friedhof means 'cemetery' (literally 'peace yard').

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hōhī,' derived from 'hoch' (high), meaning 'height,' 'altitude,' or 'level.'
examples: In welcher Höhe fliegt das Flugzeug? (At what altitude does the plane fly?)
Die Höhe des Berges beträgt 3000 Meter. (The height of the mountain is 3000 meters.)
details.usage: Used in many contexts: Höhenmeter (meters of elevation), Höhenangst (fear of heights), and in der Höhe von (in the amount of).

details.etymology: Derived from 'impfen' (to vaccinate), from Middle High German 'impfeten' (to graft), originally an agricultural term for grafting plants.
examples: Hast du schon deine Impfung bekommen? (Have you already received your vaccination?)
Die Impfung gegen Grippe ist empfehlenswert. (The flu vaccination is recommended.)
details.usage: Impfpass means 'vaccination record'; Impfpflicht means 'mandatory vaccination'; the topic became especially prominent during the COVID-19 pandemic.

details.etymology: From French 'cabine' (small room), from Italian 'cabina,' diminutive of 'caba' (cabin), ultimately from Latin 'capanna' (hut).
examples: Die Kabine im Flugzeug war sehr eng. (The cabin on the plane was very cramped.)
Wo sind die Umkleidekabinen? (Where are the changing rooms?)
details.usage: Used for airplane cabins, changing rooms (Umkleidekabine), phone booths (Telefonkabine), and ship cabins.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kaltī,' derived from 'kalt' (cold), meaning 'cold' or 'coldness' as a noun.
examples: Bei dieser Kälte bleibe ich lieber zu Hause. (In this cold I prefer to stay at home.)
Die Kälte im Winter ist manchmal unerträglich. (The cold in winter is sometimes unbearable.)
details.usage: Rarely has a plural; Kältewelle means 'cold wave' and Kälteeinbruch means 'cold snap.'

details.etymology: From Italian 'cantina' (cellar/wine shop), from Latin 'canthus' (corner), meaning a workplace cafeteria.
examples: Wir essen in der Kantine zu Mittag. (We eat lunch in the cafeteria.)
Das Essen in der Kantine ist günstig. (The food in the cafeteria is affordable.)
details.usage: Found in workplaces, schools, and military facilities; similar to Mensa (university cafeteria).

details.etymology: From Latin 'capitulum' (small head/chapter heading), diminutive of 'caput' (head), entering German through church and scholarly language.
examples: Lies bitte das nächste Kapitel. (Please read the next chapter.)
Das war ein schwieriges Kapitel in meinem Leben. (That was a difficult chapter in my life.)
details.usage: The plural is identical to the singular; used both literally for book chapters and figuratively for phases of life.

details.etymology: From Italian 'tartufolo' (truffle), because the potato, introduced to Europe in the 16th century, resembled a truffle growing underground.
examples: Kartoffeln sind ein wichtiges Grundnahrungsmittel. (Potatoes are an important staple food.)
Möchtest du Kartoffeln oder Reis? (Would you like potatoes or rice?)
details.usage: Central to German cuisine; Kartoffelsalat, Kartoffelpuffer (potato pancakes), and Bratkartoffeln (fried potatoes) are classic dishes.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kerza,' borrowed from Latin 'charta' (paper/wick) or possibly 'cereus' (waxen), referring to a wax light.
examples: Wir haben Kerzen auf den Tisch gestellt. (We placed candles on the table.)
Die Kerzen auf dem Adventskranz werden nacheinander angezündet. (The candles on the Advent wreath are lit one by one.)
details.usage: Kerzen are important in German holiday traditions, especially on the Adventskranz (Advent wreath) and Weihnachtsbaum (Christmas tree).

details.etymology: From Rotwelsch (German thieves' cant) 'kneipe' (a narrow, cramped place), meaning 'pub' or 'bar.'
examples: Heute Abend gehen wir in die Kneipe. (Tonight we are going to the pub.)
In der Altstadt gibt es viele gemütliche Kneipen. (In the old town there are many cozy pubs.)
details.usage: A Kneipe is a casual neighborhood pub, distinct from a Restaurant; Kneipentour or Kneipenbummel means 'pub crawl.'

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'koffer', borrowed from Old French 'cofre' (chest, trunk), ultimately from Latin 'cophinus' (basket).
examples: Ich habe meinen Koffer schon gepackt. (I have already packed my suitcase.)
Der Koffer ist zu schwer für das Handgepäck. (The suitcase is too heavy for carry-on luggage.)
details.usage: Commonly used in travel contexts and appears in compounds like Kofferraum (car trunk) and Handkoffer (hand luggage).

details.etymology: Feminine form of 'Kollege', borrowed from Latin 'collega' (one chosen alongside another), from 'com-' (together) + 'legare' (to choose).
examples: Meine Kollegin hilft mir bei dem Projekt. (My colleague helps me with the project.)
Die neue Kollegin kommt aus München. (The new colleague comes from Munich.)
details.usage: Used to refer specifically to a female colleague; in modern German, gender-neutral alternatives like 'Kolleg*innen' are increasingly common in formal writing.

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'comfort', which itself came from Old French 'confort' (strengthening, consolation), from Late Latin 'confortare'.
examples: Das Hotel bietet viel Komfort. (The hotel offers a lot of comfort.)
Für mehr Komfort sollten Sie die erste Klasse buchen. (For more comfort, you should book first class.)
details.usage: Typically used as an uncountable noun referring to physical comfort or luxury, and appears in compounds like Komfortwohnung (luxury apartment).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'concerto', derived from Latin 'concertare' (to compete, to arrange), entering German in the 17th century.
examples: Wir gehen heute Abend ins Konzert. (We are going to a concert tonight.)
Das Konzert fängt um 20 Uhr an. (The concert starts at 8 PM.)
details.usage: Used for both live music events and classical musical compositions, and appears in compounds like Konzerthalle (concert hall) and Konzertticket.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'korb', borrowed from Latin 'corbis' (basket).
examples: Sie hat einen Korb mit Obst gekauft. (She bought a basket of fruit.)
Er hat ihr einen Korb gegeben. (He rejected her / gave her the brush-off.)
details.usage: Beyond its literal meaning of basket, 'jemandem einen Korb geben' is a common idiom meaning to reject someone, especially romantically.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kreiz' (circle, ring), related to the verb 'kreißen' (to circle), with Germanic roots.
examples: Die Kinder sitzen im Kreis. (The children are sitting in a circle.)
Er arbeitet im Landkreis München. (He works in the Munich district.)
details.usage: Used both geometrically for circle and administratively for district (Landkreis), and appears in compounds like Kreislauf (circulation) and Kreisverkehr (roundabout).

details.etymology: From Middle Low German 'küste', borrowed from Latin 'costa' (rib, side, coast).
examples: Im Sommer fahren wir an die Küste. (In summer we drive to the coast.)
Die Küste ist sehr schön und ruhig. (The coast is very beautiful and quiet.)
details.usage: Commonly used when talking about seaside holidays, and appears in compounds like Küstenstadt (coastal city) and Nordseeküste (North Sea coast).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'leisten' (to accomplish, to perform), from Old High German 'leistjan' (to follow a track, to carry out).
examples: Seine Leistung in der Prüfung war sehr gut. (His performance on the exam was very good.)
Die Leistungen des Arztes werden von der Versicherung bezahlt. (The doctor's services are paid by insurance.)
details.usage: A versatile word meaning performance, achievement, or service, central to German work culture and found in compounds like Leistungsdruck (performance pressure).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'liefern' (to deliver), from Middle High German 'līfern', borrowed from Middle Dutch 'liveren'.
examples: Die Lieferung kommt morgen an. (The delivery arrives tomorrow.)
Wir bieten kostenlose Lieferung ab 50 Euro. (We offer free delivery from 50 euros.)
details.usage: Very common in e-commerce and logistics contexts, and appears in compounds like Lieferzeit (delivery time) and Lieferschein (delivery note).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'lōn' (reward, wages), with ancient Germanic roots shared with English 'loan' in its older sense of reward.
examples: Der Lohn wird am Ende des Monats gezahlt. (The wages are paid at the end of the month.)
Für diese Arbeit bekommt man einen guten Lohn. (For this work one gets good wages.)
details.usage: Typically refers to hourly or manual labor wages (as opposed to 'Gehalt' for salaried pay), and appears in compounds like Mindestlohn (minimum wage) and Lohnsteuer (wage tax).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'luft' (air), with Germanic roots related to the concept of sky and air.
examples: Die Luft hier in den Bergen ist sehr frisch. (The air here in the mountains is very fresh.)
Ich brauche frische Luft. (I need fresh air.)
details.usage: Used in everyday speech and in many compounds like Luftballon (balloon), Luftverschmutzung (air pollution), and the idiom 'jemanden wie Luft behandeln' (to give someone the cold shoulder).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Mahl' (meal, from Old High German 'māl' meaning time or occasion) and 'Zeit' (time), literally meaning 'mealtime'.
examples: Wir haben drei Mahlzeiten am Tag. (We have three meals a day.)
Mahlzeit! Wie geht es Ihnen? (Enjoy your meal! How are you?)
details.usage: Besides meaning meal, 'Mahlzeit!' is commonly used as an informal greeting around lunchtime in German workplaces.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mangal' (lack, deficiency), related to Latin 'mancus' (maimed, defective).
examples: Es gibt einen Mangel an Fachkräften. (There is a shortage of skilled workers.)
Die Wohnung hat einige Mängel. (The apartment has some defects.)
details.usage: Used both for shortage/lack ('Mangel an') and for defects/flaws, and appears in compounds like Fachkräftemangel (skilled labor shortage) and mangelhaft (deficient).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'machine', from Latin 'machina', from Greek 'mēkhanē' (device, contrivance).
examples: Die Maschine funktioniert nicht mehr. (The machine no longer works.)
Unsere Maschine nach Berlin startet um 14 Uhr. (Our plane to Berlin departs at 2 PM.)
details.usage: Can mean both machine and airplane (colloquially), and appears in compounds like Waschmaschine (washing machine) and Kaffeemaschine (coffee maker).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'materialis' (relating to matter), from 'materia' (matter, substance, timber).
examples: Wir brauchen mehr Material für den Bau. (We need more material for the construction.)
Das Material ist von hoher Qualität. (The material is of high quality.)
details.usage: Used for both physical materials and educational/work materials (Unterrichtsmaterial = teaching material, Büromaterial = office supplies).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mūra', borrowed from Latin 'mūrus' (wall), entering Germanic languages during the Roman period.
examples: Die Berliner Mauer fiel 1989. (The Berlin Wall fell in 1989.)
Um den Garten steht eine alte Mauer. (An old wall stands around the garden.)
details.usage: Strongly associated with the Berlin Wall (Berliner Mauer) in cultural memory; differs from 'Wand' (interior wall) in that Mauer refers to a thick, solid, or exterior wall.

details.etymology: Formed from 'mehr' (more) + '-heit' (abstract noun suffix), meaning the greater part or majority.
examples: Die Mehrheit hat für den Vorschlag gestimmt. (The majority voted for the proposal.)
Die große Mehrheit der Bevölkerung ist dagegen. (The vast majority of the population is against it.)
details.usage: Frequently used in political and democratic contexts, and contrasts with 'Minderheit' (minority).

details.etymology: Derived from 'Metzger' (butcher), from Middle High German 'metzjer', likely from Latin 'macellarius' (butcher), with the suffix '-ei' denoting a shop.
examples: Ich kaufe das Fleisch immer in der Metzgerei. (I always buy meat at the butcher shop.)
Die Metzgerei um die Ecke hat sehr gute Wurst. (The butcher shop around the corner has very good sausage.)
details.usage: Primarily used in southern Germany and Switzerland; in northern Germany, the equivalent is 'Fleischerei' or 'Schlachterei'.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Mittag' (midday, from 'Mitte' + 'Tag') and 'Essen' (food/eating), literally meaning 'midday meal'.
examples: Das Mittagessen ist um 12 Uhr fertig. (Lunch is ready at 12 o'clock.)
Was gibt es heute zum Mittagessen? (What is there for lunch today?)
details.usage: Lunch is traditionally the main warm meal of the day in Germany, and the word parallels Frühstück (breakfast) and Abendessen (dinner).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'meuble' (furniture), from Latin 'mobilis' (movable), since furniture consists of movable household items.
examples: Wir brauchen neue Möbel für das Wohnzimmer. (We need new furniture for the living room.)
Die Möbel sind aus Holz. (The furniture is made of wood.)
details.usage: Almost always used in the plural in German, and appears in compounds like Möbelhaus (furniture store) and Möbelstück (piece of furniture).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'muoh' (effort, trouble), with ancient Germanic roots.
examples: Er hat sich viel Mühe gegeben. (He made a lot of effort.)
Es ist die Mühe wert. (It is worth the effort.)
details.usage: Often used in the expression 'sich Mühe geben' (to make an effort) and in the formal phrase 'Vielen Dank für Ihre Mühe' (Thank you for your trouble).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'mül' (dust, rubbish), related to the verb 'mahlen' (to grind), originally referring to ground-up waste.
examples: Bitte bring den Müll raus! (Please take the trash out!)
In Deutschland wird der Müll getrennt. (In Germany, waste is separated.)
details.usage: Central to German culture due to strict recycling rules (Mülltrennung), and appears in compounds like Mülltonne (garbage bin), Müllabfuhr (garbage collection), and Sperrmüll (bulky waste).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'munizza', borrowed from Latin 'moneta' (mint, money), named after the Roman goddess Juno Moneta whose temple housed a mint.
examples: Haben Sie eine Münze für den Einkaufswagen? (Do you have a coin for the shopping cart?)
Er sammelt alte Münzen. (He collects old coins.)
details.usage: Used for physical coins as opposed to paper money (Schein), and appears in compounds like Münzautomat (coin-operated machine).

details.etymology: A compound of 'nach' (after, behind) and 'Teil' (part), meaning the disadvantageous part or downside.
examples: Ein Nachteil dieser Wohnung ist der Lärm. (A disadvantage of this apartment is the noise.)
Jede Entscheidung hat Vor- und Nachteile. (Every decision has advantages and disadvantages.)
details.usage: Commonly paired with its antonym 'Vorteil' (advantage) in discussions comparing pros and cons.

context: In der ___ gibt es einen Park.

details.etymology: Derived from the adjective 'nah' (near, close), from Old High German 'nāh', with the abstract noun suffix '-e'.
examples: Gibt es ein Restaurant in der Nähe? (Is there a restaurant nearby?)
Die Nähe zum Bahnhof ist ein Vorteil. (The proximity to the train station is an advantage.)
details.usage: Most commonly used in the prepositional phrase 'in der Nähe von' (near, in the vicinity of).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'nuodel', likely from an early Germanic form meaning a lump of dough, though some scholars suggest a connection to Latin 'nodus' (knot).
examples: Kinder essen gerne Nudeln mit Soße. (Children like to eat noodles with sauce.)
Die Nudeln müssen noch zehn Minuten kochen. (The noodles need to cook for ten more minutes.)
details.usage: Almost always used in the plural (Nudeln), and covers all types of pasta and noodles in German, with compounds like Nudelsalat (pasta salad) and Nudelsuppe (noodle soup).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'packen' (to pack), with the suffix '-ung' forming an action/result noun.
examples: Ich brauche eine Packung Mehl. (I need a packet of flour.)
Auf der Packung steht das Haltbarkeitsdatum. (The expiration date is on the package.)
details.usage: Used for packages, packets, and cartons of goods, and colloquially 'eine Packung bekommen' means to get a beating or suffer a heavy defeat.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'pfanna', borrowed from Latin 'patina' (pan, dish), which came from Greek 'patanē'.
examples: Brate das Fleisch in der Pfanne. (Fry the meat in the pan.)
Ich habe eine neue Pfanne gekauft. (I bought a new frying pan.)
details.usage: Refers specifically to a frying pan (as opposed to 'Topf' for a pot), and appears in compounds like Bratpfanne (frying pan) and Pfannkuchen (pancake).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'pflanza', borrowed from Latin 'planta' (sprout, plant).
examples: Die Pflanze braucht mehr Wasser. (The plant needs more water.)
Sie hat viele Pflanzen auf dem Balkon. (She has many plants on the balcony.)
details.usage: A general term for any plant, and appears in compounds like Zimmerpflanze (houseplant), Pflanzenschutz (plant protection), and Heilpflanze (medicinal plant).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'plaz', borrowed from Old French 'place', from Latin 'platea' (broad street), from Greek 'plateia' (broad way).
examples: Ist dieser Platz noch frei? (Is this seat still free?)
Wir treffen uns am Marktplatz. (We meet at the market square.)
details.usage: Highly versatile, meaning seat, space, square, or court depending on context, and appears in many compounds like Parkplatz (parking space), Spielplatz (playground), and Arbeitsplatz (workplace).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Post' (mail, from Italian 'posta', from Latin 'posita' meaning stationed) and 'Amt' (office).
examples: Das Postamt ist um die Ecke. (The post office is around the corner.)
Ich muss zum Postamt, um ein Paket abzuholen. (I have to go to the post office to pick up a package.)
details.usage: Traditional term for post office, though many Postämter in Germany have been replaced by postal counters inside shops (Postfiliale) since the privatization of Deutsche Post.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'practicum', from Greek 'praktikon' (practical thing), related to 'praxis' (practice).
examples: Ich mache im Sommer ein Praktikum bei Siemens. (I am doing an internship at Siemens in summer.)
Das Praktikum dauert drei Monate. (The internship lasts three months.)
details.usage: Internships are extremely common in Germany for students and career changers, and the person doing one is called a Praktikant (m.) or Praktikantin (f.).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Greek 'praxis' (action, practice), from 'prassein' (to do, to act).
examples: Die Praxis von Dr. Müller ist heute geschlossen. (Dr. Müller's practice is closed today.)
In der Praxis sieht das anders aus. (In practice, that looks different.)
details.usage: Has two main meanings: a doctor's or therapist's office (Arztpraxis), and practical application as opposed to theory (Theorie und Praxis).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'rabatto' (rebate, discount), from 'rabattere' (to beat down), ultimately from Latin 're-' + 'abattere'.
examples: Studenten bekommen 10 Prozent Rabatt. (Students get a 10 percent discount.)
Es gibt heute einen Rabatt auf alle Schuhe. (There is a discount on all shoes today.)
details.usage: Widely used in shopping and retail contexts, and appears in compounds like Rabattaktion (discount promotion) and Mengenrabatt (bulk discount).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Rad' (wheel/bicycle, from Old High German 'rad') and 'Tour' (tour, from French 'tour').
examples: Am Wochenende machen wir eine Radtour. (On the weekend we are going on a bike tour.)
Die Radtour entlang der Donau war wunderschön. (The bike tour along the Danube was wonderful.)
details.usage: Germany has an extensive network of bike paths (Radwege), making Radtouren a very popular leisure activity.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'regula', borrowed from Latin 'regula' (straight stick, rule), from 'regere' (to direct).
examples: Man muss die Regeln beachten. (One must follow the rules.)
In der Regel bin ich um 8 Uhr im Büro. (As a rule, I am in the office at 8 o'clock.)
details.usage: Used for rules and regulations, and the phrase 'in der Regel' (as a rule, usually) is very common in everyday speech.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'rīga' (row, line), with Germanic roots related to arranging things in order.
examples: Wir sitzen in der dritten Reihe. (We are sitting in the third row.)
Eine ganze Reihe von Problemen muss gelöst werden. (A whole series of problems must be solved.)
details.usage: Means both a physical row and a series/sequence, and the idiom 'an der Reihe sein' means it is one's turn.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'reinigen' (to clean), from 'rein' (clean, pure) + '-igung' suffix, from Old High German 'hreini'.
examples: Ich bringe meinen Anzug in die Reinigung. (I am taking my suit to the dry cleaner.)
Die Reinigung des Gebäudes dauert zwei Stunden. (The cleaning of the building takes two hours.)
details.usage: Refers both to the act of cleaning and to a dry cleaning shop (chemische Reinigung), commonly shortened to just 'Reinigung' in everyday speech.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Rind' (cattle, from Old High German 'rind') and 'Fleisch' (meat, from Old High German 'fleisc').
examples: Rindfleisch ist teurer als Schweinefleisch. (Beef is more expensive than pork.)
Für dieses Rezept brauchen wir 500 Gramm Rindfleisch. (For this recipe we need 500 grams of beef.)
details.usage: A straightforward compound typical of German food vocabulary; the famously long word 'Rindfleischetikettierungsüberwachungsaufgabenübertragungsgesetz' was an actual German law name that began with this word.

details.etymology: A compound of 'rück-' (back, from 'zurück') and 'Fahrt' (journey, trip), from Old High German 'fart'.
examples: Die Rückfahrt dauert zwei Stunden. (The return trip takes two hours.)
Hast du schon ein Ticket für die Rückfahrt? (Do you already have a ticket for the return trip?)
details.usage: Commonly paired with 'Hinfahrt' (outward journey), and a 'Hin- und Rückfahrt' ticket is a round trip.

details.etymology: From Middle Low German 'sahne', related to Old French 'saime' (cream, fat), with disputed further origins.
examples: Möchtest du Sahne in den Kaffee? (Would you like cream in the coffee?)
Der Kuchen wird mit Sahne serviert. (The cake is served with cream.)
details.usage: Refers to cream in cooking and baking contexts; in southern Germany and Austria, 'Rahm' or 'Obers' are used instead, and compounds include Schlagsahne (whipped cream) and Sahnetorte (cream cake).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'sammeln' (to collect), from Old High German 'samanōn', with the '-ung' suffix forming a noun.
examples: Das Museum hat eine große Sammlung von Gemälden. (The museum has a large collection of paintings.)
Seine Briefmarkensammlung ist sehr wertvoll. (His stamp collection is very valuable.)
details.usage: Used for collections of art, stamps, data, and more, and also for the act of collecting or gathering (Spendensammlung = fundraising collection).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'skiht' (layer, arrangement), related to the verb 'schichten' (to layer, to stack).
examples: Er arbeitet in der Nachtschicht. (He works the night shift.)
Der Kuchen hat drei Schichten. (The cake has three layers.)
details.usage: Has two main meanings: a physical layer/stratum and a work shift (Frühschicht = early shift, Spätschicht = late shift), also used in sociology for social class (soziale Schicht).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'slango', related to the verb 'schlingen' (to wind, to coil), describing the animal's movement.
examples: Vor der Kasse ist eine lange Schlange. (There is a long queue at the checkout.)
Im Zoo haben wir eine große Schlange gesehen. (At the zoo we saw a large snake.)
details.usage: Means both snake and queue/line; 'Schlange stehen' (to stand in line) is a very common expression in daily German life.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'snēo', from Proto-Germanic '*snaiwaz', related to English 'snow' and Latin 'nix, nivis'.
examples: Im Winter liegt viel Schnee in den Bergen. (In winter there is a lot of snow in the mountains.)
Die Kinder spielen gern im Schnee. (The children like to play in the snow.)
details.usage: Used as an uncountable noun, and appears in compounds like Schneemann (snowman), Schneeflocke (snowflake), and Schneefall (snowfall).

details.etymology: A diminutive of 'Schnitz' (a cut piece), from the verb 'schnitzen' (to carve), ultimately from Old High German 'snītan' (to cut).
examples: Ich bestelle ein Wiener Schnitzel. (I'll order a Wiener Schnitzel.)
Das Schnitzel mit Pommes ist sehr lecker. (The schnitzel with fries is very tasty.)
details.usage: One of the most iconic German and Austrian dishes; Wiener Schnitzel is traditionally made from veal, while Schweineschnitzel (pork schnitzel) is the everyday variant.

details.etymology: A compound of 'schwimmen' (to swim, from Old High German 'swimman') and 'Bad' (bath, pool).
examples: Im Sommer gehen wir oft ins Schwimmbad. (In summer we often go to the swimming pool.)
Das Schwimmbad hat von 9 bis 20 Uhr geöffnet. (The swimming pool is open from 9 AM to 8 PM.)
details.usage: Refers to a public swimming pool, with variants like Freibad (outdoor pool), Hallenbad (indoor pool), and the more modern term Erlebnisbad (adventure pool/water park).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'semestris' (six-monthly), from 'sex' (six) + 'mensis' (month).
examples: Das neue Semester beginnt im Oktober. (The new semester begins in October.)
Sie ist im dritten Semester. (She is in her third semester.)
details.usage: German universities use the semester system with Wintersemester (October-March) and Sommersemester (April-September), and Semesterferien refers to the semester break.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'sauce', from Latin 'salsa' (salted), from 'sal' (salt); the German spelling reflects pronunciation.
examples: Die Soße schmeckt sehr gut. (The sauce tastes very good.)
Möchtest du mehr Soße auf dem Fleisch? (Would you like more sauce on the meat?)
details.usage: The spelling 'Soße' is standard German (Duden), while 'Sauce' is also accepted; appears in compounds like Tomatensoße (tomato sauce) and Bratsoße (gravy).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Spiel' (play, game, from Old High German 'spil') and 'Platz' (place, square).
examples: Die Kinder spielen auf dem Spielplatz. (The children are playing on the playground.)
In der Nähe gibt es einen großen Spielplatz. (Nearby there is a large playground.)
details.usage: Playgrounds are very common in German residential areas and are typically well-maintained by local municipalities.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Sport' (sport, borrowed from English) and 'Halle' (hall, from Old High German 'halla').
examples: Der Sportunterricht findet in der Sporthalle statt. (Physical education takes place in the sports hall.)
Die Sporthalle wird gerade renoviert. (The sports hall is currently being renovated.)
details.usage: Nearly every German school has a Sporthalle for physical education and community sports events, also called Turnhalle.

details.etymology: A compound of 'stecken' (to stick, plug in) and 'Dose' (box, socket), literally a 'plug box'.
examples: Wo ist hier eine Steckdose? (Where is an outlet here?)
Die Steckdose funktioniert nicht. (The power outlet doesn't work.)
details.usage: German Steckdosen use the Type F (Schuko) plug standard, and the counterpart you plug into it is called a 'Stecker' (plug).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stella' (place, position), related to the verb 'stellen' (to place, to put).
examples: An dieser Stelle stand früher ein Baum. (A tree used to stand at this spot.)
Sie hat eine neue Stelle als Lehrerin bekommen. (She got a new position as a teacher.)
details.usage: Very versatile: means spot/place, job position (Arbeitsstelle), decimal point (Dezimalstelle), and passage in a text, also used in compounds like Stellenanzeige (job advertisement).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stempfil' (stamping tool), related to 'stampfen' (to stamp), with Germanic roots.
examples: Ich brauche einen Stempel in meinem Pass. (I need a stamp in my passport.)
Der Brief hat keinen Stempel. (The letter has no stamp/postmark.)
details.usage: Stamps and official seals remain important in German bureaucracy; 'stempeln gehen' is a colloquial expression meaning to be unemployed (go to the unemployment office).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Stock' (stick, story/floor, from Old High German 'stoc') and 'Werk' (work, structure), referring to a building level.
examples: Das Büro ist im dritten Stockwerk. (The office is on the third floor.)
Das Gebäude hat fünf Stockwerke. (The building has five floors.)
details.usage: Synonymous with 'Etage' and 'Geschoss'; note that the ground floor in Germany is 'Erdgeschoss' (EG), so the first Stockwerk corresponds to the second floor in American English.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'stören' (to disturb), from Old High German 'stōrēn', with the '-ung' suffix.
examples: Es gibt eine Störung im Telefonnetz. (There is a disruption in the phone network.)
Entschuldigen Sie die Störung. (Sorry for the disturbance.)
details.usage: Used for technical faults, interruptions, and disturbances, and also in medical/psychological contexts for disorders (Schlafstörung = sleep disorder).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'teppich', borrowed via Old French 'tapiz' from Byzantine Greek 'tapētion', diminutive of 'tapēs' (carpet).
examples: Der Teppich im Wohnzimmer ist sehr weich. (The carpet in the living room is very soft.)
Wir müssen den Teppich reinigen lassen. (We need to have the carpet cleaned.)
details.usage: Covers both wall-to-wall carpet (Teppichboden) and area rugs, and the idiom 'etwas unter den Teppich kehren' means to sweep something under the rug.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'tor' (gate), with ancient Germanic roots; distinct from 'Tür' (door) in referring to a larger gate or goal.
examples: Das Tor zum Schloss ist geschlossen. (The gate to the castle is closed.)
Er hat ein Tor geschossen! (He scored a goal!)
details.usage: Means both a large gate and a goal in sports (especially soccer), and appears in compounds like Torwart (goalkeeper) and Stadttor (city gate).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'troum', from Proto-Germanic '*draumaz', related to English 'dream'.
examples: Ich hatte letzte Nacht einen schönen Traum. (I had a beautiful dream last night.)
Mein Traum ist es, eine Weltreise zu machen. (My dream is to travel around the world.)
details.usage: Used for both sleeping dreams and aspirational dreams, and appears in compounds like Traumjob (dream job), Traumhaus (dream house), and Albtraum (nightmare).

details.etymology: A compound of 'trinken' (to drink) and 'Geld' (money), literally 'drinking money', originally money given to buy a drink.
examples: In Deutschland gibt man etwa 5 bis 10 Prozent Trinkgeld. (In Germany you tip about 5 to 10 percent.)
Hast du dem Kellner Trinkgeld gegeben? (Did you give the waiter a tip?)
details.usage: Tipping in Germany is customary but less than in the US; it is typically done by rounding up the bill or adding 5-10%, and one says 'Stimmt so' (keep the change) when paying.

details.etymology: From Middle Low German 'tüte' (cone, cornet), possibly of onomatopoeic origin imitating a horn shape.
examples: Brauchen Sie eine Tüte? (Do you need a bag?)
Er hat eine Tüte Chips gekauft. (He bought a bag of chips.)
details.usage: In northern Germany, Tüte means a (plastic or paper) bag, while in southern Germany 'Beutel' or 'Sackerl' (Austria) are more common; since 2022 single-use plastic Tüten are banned in Germany.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'übersetzen' (to translate), a calque of Latin 'traducere' (to lead across), with 'über' (over) + 'setzen' (to set).
examples: Die Übersetzung des Buches ist sehr gut. (The translation of the book is very good.)
Können Sie mir bei der Übersetzung helfen? (Can you help me with the translation?)
details.usage: Used for written translations (as opposed to 'Dolmetschen' for interpreting), and appears in compounds like Übersetzungsbüro (translation agency).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'üben' (to practice), from Old High German 'uoben', with the '-ung' suffix.
examples: Übung macht den Meister. (Practice makes perfect.)
Machen Sie bitte Übung drei auf Seite 20. (Please do exercise three on page 20.)
details.usage: Means both practice and exercise (in textbooks, sports, etc.), and the proverb 'Übung macht den Meister' is one of the most well-known German sayings.

details.etymology: A compound of the prefix 'un-' (negative) and 'Fall' (fall, case), from Old High German, meaning an unfortunate event.
examples: Auf der Autobahn gab es einen schweren Unfall. (There was a serious accident on the highway.)
Nach dem Unfall wurde er ins Krankenhaus gebracht. (After the accident he was taken to the hospital.)
details.usage: Used primarily for traffic accidents and workplace injuries, and appears in compounds like Unfallversicherung (accident insurance) and Unfallbericht (accident report).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'verbinden' (to bind, to connect), from Old High German 'firbintan'.
examples: Die Krankenschwester hat einen Verband angelegt. (The nurse applied a bandage.)
Er ist Mitglied in einem Sportverband. (He is a member of a sports association.)
details.usage: Has two distinct meanings: a medical bandage and an association/federation (like Verband der Automobilindustrie), reflecting the dual sense of 'verbinden' (to bind/connect).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'verbieten' (to forbid), from Old High German 'firbiotan', with the prefix 'ver-' intensifying 'bieten' (to offer/command).
examples: In der Schule gibt es ein Handyverbot. (There is a cell phone ban at school.)
Das Verbot gilt ab sofort. (The ban takes effect immediately.)
details.usage: Germany is known for its regulations, and Verbot appears in many compounds like Rauchverbot (smoking ban), Fahrverbot (driving ban), and Parkverbot (no parking).

details.etymology: From the reflexive verb 'sich verhalten' (to behave), from Old High German 'firhaltan' (to hold back, to conduct oneself).
examples: Sein Verhalten war sehr unhöflich. (His behavior was very rude.)
Das Verhalten der Kinder in der Schule war vorbildlich. (The children's behavior at school was exemplary.)
details.usage: Used as an uncountable noun for behavior/conduct, and appears in compounds like Verhaltensregel (rule of conduct) and Fehlverhalten (misconduct).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'versichern' (to insure, to assure), from 'ver-' + 'sicher' (safe, sure), with the '-ung' suffix.
examples: Die Krankenversicherung ist in Deutschland Pflicht. (Health insurance is mandatory in Germany.)
Haben Sie eine Versicherung für Ihr Auto? (Do you have insurance for your car?)
details.usage: Insurance is a major part of German life; common types include Krankenversicherung (health), Haftpflichtversicherung (liability), and Rentenversicherung (pension).

details.etymology: A compound of 'vor' (before, in front of) and 'Hang' (related to 'hängen', to hang), literally something hanging in front.
examples: Kannst du bitte die Vorhänge zuziehen? (Can you please close the curtains?)
Die neuen Vorhänge sind aus Baumwolle. (The new curtains are made of cotton.)
details.usage: Refers to curtains or drapes in homes and also to the theater curtain, with the expression 'der Vorhang fällt' (the curtain falls) meaning the show is over.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'vorstellen' (to present, to introduce, to imagine), from 'vor' (before) + 'stellen' (to place).
examples: Die nächste Vorstellung beginnt um 19 Uhr. (The next performance begins at 7 PM.)
Ich habe keine Vorstellung davon, wie das funktioniert. (I have no idea how that works.)
details.usage: Has multiple meanings: a theater performance, an idea/concept, and an introduction (Vorstellungsgespräch = job interview, literally 'introduction conversation').

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wāga' (scales, balance), related to the verb 'wägen' (to weigh) and English 'weigh'.
examples: Stell dich mal auf die Waage. (Step on the scale.)
Die Waage zeigt 70 Kilo an. (The scale shows 70 kilos.)
details.usage: Refers to any type of scale or balance, and is also the German name for the zodiac sign Libra; compounds include Küchenwaage (kitchen scale) and Briefwaage (letter scale).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wald' (forest), from Proto-Germanic '*walþuz', related to English 'wold' (open hilly area).
examples: Am Sonntag gehen wir im Wald spazieren. (On Sunday we go for a walk in the forest.)
Der Schwarzwald liegt in Süddeutschland. (The Black Forest is in southern Germany.)
details.usage: Forests hold deep cultural significance in Germany (about a third of the country is forested), and the word appears in compounds like Regenwald (rainforest) and Waldweg (forest path).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wasca', derived from 'waskan' (to wash), meaning things to be washed or clothing/linen.
examples: Ich muss heute Wäsche waschen. (I have to do laundry today.)
Die Wäsche hängt draußen zum Trocknen. (The laundry is hanging outside to dry.)
details.usage: Refers to both laundry and underwear/lingerie (Unterwäsche), and appears in compounds like Wäscheleine (clothesline), Wäschetrockner (dryer), and Bettwäsche (bed linen).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Werk' (work, from Old High German 'werc') and 'Zeug' (stuff, equipment), literally 'work stuff'.
examples: Hast du das richtige Werkzeug für die Reparatur? (Do you have the right tool for the repair?)
Der Werkzeugkasten ist in der Garage. (The toolbox is in the garage.)
details.usage: Can refer to a single tool or tools collectively, and appears in compounds like Werkzeugkasten (toolbox) and Werkzeugmaschine (machine tool).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wisa' (meadow), with ancient Germanic roots related to moist grassland.
examples: Die Kinder spielen auf der Wiese. (The children are playing on the meadow.)
Im Frühling blühen viele Blumen auf der Wiese. (In spring many flowers bloom on the meadow.)
details.usage: A common landscape feature in Germany, and the famous Munich Oktoberfest takes place on the Theresienwiese, often shortened to Wiesn in Bavarian dialect.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wolka' (cloud), from Proto-Germanic '*wulkō', possibly related to 'welk' (damp).
examples: Heute sind viele Wolken am Himmel. (Today there are many clouds in the sky.)
Er schwebt auf Wolke sieben. (He is on cloud nine.)
details.usage: The idiom 'auf Wolke sieben schweben' (to float on cloud seven) means to be extremely happy, and compounds include Wolkenkratzer (skyscraper, literally cloud scratcher).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wunta' (wound, injury), from Proto-Germanic '*wundō', related to English 'wound'.
examples: Die Wunde muss desinfiziert werden. (The wound needs to be disinfected.)
Die Wunde ist schon fast verheilt. (The wound has almost healed already.)
details.usage: Used in medical contexts and figuratively for emotional wounds, and the expression 'Salz in die Wunde streuen' (to rub salt in the wound) is a common idiom.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zūn' (fence, enclosure), from Proto-Germanic '*tūnaz', related to English 'town' (originally meaning enclosed area).
examples: Der Nachbar hat einen neuen Zaun gebaut. (The neighbor built a new fence.)
Der Hund springt über den Zaun. (The dog jumps over the fence.)
details.usage: Used for fences and enclosures, and the idiom 'einen Streit vom Zaun brechen' means to pick a fight or start a quarrel.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zīla' (row, line), related to the concept of arranging things in sequence.
examples: Lesen Sie bitte die dritte Zeile. (Please read the third line.)
Ich schreibe dir nur ein paar Zeilen. (I'm just writing you a few lines.)
details.usage: Used for lines of text, rows, and in computing for lines of code (Codezeile), and 'zwischen den Zeilen lesen' means to read between the lines.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Zeit' (time) and 'Schrift' (writing, publication), literally a 'time-writing' or periodic publication.
examples: Ich lese gerne Zeitschriften im Wartezimmer. (I like to read magazines in the waiting room.)
Diese Zeitschrift erscheint einmal im Monat. (This magazine is published once a month.)
details.usage: Refers to magazines and journals (as opposed to Zeitung for newspapers), and compounds include Fachzeitschrift (specialist journal) and Zeitschriftenständer (magazine rack).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Zeit' (time) and the suffix '-ung', originally meaning tidings or news, first attested in the 14th century.
examples: Ich lese jeden Morgen die Zeitung. (I read the newspaper every morning.)
Hast du die Zeitung von heute schon gelesen? (Have you already read today's newspaper?)
details.usage: Refers specifically to newspapers (as opposed to Zeitschrift for magazines), and appears in compounds like Tageszeitung (daily newspaper) and Zeitungsartikel (newspaper article).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'zetel', borrowed from Italian 'cedola' (slip of paper), from Latin 'schedula' (small strip of papyrus).
examples: Ich habe dir einen Zettel auf den Tisch gelegt. (I left you a note on the table.)
Schreib es auf einen Zettel, damit du es nicht vergisst. (Write it on a piece of paper so you don't forget it.)
details.usage: A casual word for a slip or piece of paper, and appears in compounds like Notizzettel (note), Einkaufszettel (shopping list), and Strafzettel (parking ticket).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zimbar' (timber, building material, room), originally meaning timber or a structure built of wood.
examples: Das Hotel hat noch freie Zimmer. (The hotel still has available rooms.)
Mein Zimmer ist nicht sehr groß. (My room is not very big.)
details.usage: A general word for room, and appears in many compounds like Schlafzimmer (bedroom), Wohnzimmer (living room), Badezimmer (bathroom), and Einzelzimmer (single room).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'zucker', borrowed via Italian 'zucchero' from Arabic 'sukkar', ultimately from Sanskrit 'śarkarā' (grit, gravel, sugar).
examples: Möchtest du Zucker in den Tee? (Would you like sugar in the tea?)
Der Arzt sagt, ich soll weniger Zucker essen. (The doctor says I should eat less sugar.)
details.usage: Used as an uncountable noun, and appears in compounds like Zuckerwatte (cotton candy), Puderzucker (powdered sugar), and colloquially 'Zucker haben' meaning to have diabetes.

details.etymology: A compound of 'zu' (to) and 'Gang' (going, passage), from the verb 'zugehen' (to go towards), literally meaning an approach or entrance.
examples: Der Zugang zum Gebäude ist nur mit Ausweis möglich. (Access to the building is only possible with an ID.)
Alle Studenten haben Zugang zur Bibliothek. (All students have access to the library.)
details.usage: Used for both physical access (entrances) and abstract access (to information, services), and appears in compounds like Internetzugang (internet access).

details.etymology: A compound of 'zu' (to) and 'Stand' (state, position), from 'stehen' (to stand), meaning the way something stands or is.
examples: Das Auto ist in einem guten Zustand. (The car is in good condition.)
Der Zustand des Patienten hat sich verbessert. (The patient's condition has improved.)
details.usage: Used for conditions, states, and situations; colloquially 'Zustände bekommen' means to have a fit or to be horrified.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'abschließen' (to conclude, to close off), from 'ab' (off) + 'schließen' (to close).
examples: Sie hat einen guten Abschluss gemacht. (She got a good degree.)
Der Abschluss des Vertrages ist für nächste Woche geplant. (The conclusion of the contract is planned for next week.)
details.usage: Commonly refers to academic degrees (Schulabschluss, Hochschulabschluss) and the completion or conclusion of contracts and projects.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'anbieten' (to offer), from 'an' (at, on) + 'bieten' (to offer), with the past participle form 'geboten'.
examples: Das Angebot im Supermarkt ist diese Woche sehr gut. (The offers at the supermarket are very good this week.)
Er hat mir ein Angebot gemacht, das ich nicht ablehnen konnte. (He made me an offer I couldn't refuse.)
details.usage: Used for commercial offers, special deals, and supply (in economic terms: Angebot und Nachfrage = supply and demand), and appears in compounds like Sonderangebot (special offer).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'anmelden' (to register, to sign up), from 'an' (at) + 'melden' (to report).
examples: Die Anmeldung für den Kurs endet am Freitag. (Registration for the course ends on Friday.)
Bitte füllen Sie das Formular für die Anmeldung aus. (Please fill out the registration form.)
details.usage: Essential in German bureaucracy, particularly the Wohnsitzanmeldung (residence registration), which is mandatory within two weeks of moving to a new address in Germany.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'antragen' (to propose, to apply), from 'an' (at) + 'tragen' (to carry).
examples: Ich muss einen Antrag auf ein Visum stellen. (I have to submit a visa application.)
Der Antrag wurde leider abgelehnt. (The application was unfortunately rejected.)
details.usage: Central to German bureaucracy; 'einen Antrag stellen' (to file an application) is used for visas, permits, benefits, and official requests of all kinds.

details.etymology: From the adjective 'arg' (bad, wicked), from Old High German 'arg', with the comparative suffix '-er' that became a standalone noun meaning annoyance.
examples: Es gibt immer Ärger mit den Nachbarn. (There is always trouble with the neighbors.)
Mach keinen Ärger! (Don't cause any trouble!)
details.usage: Used as an uncountable noun meaning anger, annoyance, or trouble, and the related verb 'ärgern' (to annoy) is used reflexively as 'sich ärgern über' (to be annoyed about).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'ausstellen' (to exhibit, to display), from 'aus' (out) + 'stellen' (to place).
examples: Die Ausstellung im Museum ist sehr interessant. (The exhibition at the museum is very interesting.)
Die Ausstellung dauert bis Ende März. (The exhibition runs until the end of March.)
details.usage: Means both an exhibition/display and the issuing of documents (Ausstellung eines Passes = issuing of a passport).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'bedeuten' (to mean, to signify), from Middle High German 'bediuten', with the '-ung' suffix.
examples: Was ist die Bedeutung dieses Wortes? (What is the meaning of this word?)
Diese Entdeckung hat eine große Bedeutung für die Medizin. (This discovery has great significance for medicine.)
details.usage: Means both meaning/definition and significance/importance, and appears in compounds like Wortbedeutung (word meaning) and bedeutungslos (meaningless).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'beitragen' (to contribute), from 'bei' (by, with) + 'tragen' (to carry).
examples: Der monatliche Beitrag für den Verein beträgt 20 Euro. (The monthly membership fee for the club is 20 euros.)
Sein Beitrag zur Diskussion war sehr wertvoll. (His contribution to the discussion was very valuable.)
details.usage: Means both a financial contribution/fee (Mitgliedsbeitrag = membership fee, Versicherungsbeitrag = insurance premium) and a contribution in the sense of an article or post.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'beraten' (to advise, to counsel), from 'be-' + 'raten' (to advise), with the '-ung' suffix.
examples: Die Beratung im Geschäft war sehr hilfreich. (The advice in the store was very helpful.)
Ich brauche eine Beratung zum Thema Versicherung. (I need advice on the topic of insurance.)
details.usage: Used for professional consultation and advisory services, and appears in compounds like Beratungsstelle (advice center) and Beratungsgespräch (consultation meeting).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'beriht' (instruction, account), derived from the verb 'berichten' (to report), from 'be-' + 'richten' (to direct).
examples: Der Bericht über den Unfall steht in der Zeitung. (The report about the accident is in the newspaper.)
Ich muss bis morgen einen Bericht schreiben. (I have to write a report by tomorrow.)
details.usage: Used for reports of all kinds, from news to business, and appears in compounds like Wetterbericht (weather report), Jahresbericht (annual report), and Erfahrungsbericht (experience report).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'bescheit' (information, decision), derived from 'bescheiden' (to inform, to decide).
examples: Sag mir bitte Bescheid, wenn du ankommst. (Please let me know when you arrive.)
Ich warte noch auf den Bescheid vom Amt. (I'm still waiting for the notification from the office.)
details.usage: The phrase 'Bescheid sagen/geben' (to let someone know) is extremely common in everyday German, and officially a Bescheid is a formal administrative decision or notice.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'betreiben' (to operate, to run), from 'be-' + 'treiben' (to drive).
examples: Der Betrieb hat 50 Mitarbeiter. (The company has 50 employees.)
Die Maschine ist außer Betrieb. (The machine is out of service.)
details.usage: Means both a business/company (especially smaller enterprises) and operation/service; 'außer Betrieb' (out of order) is a sign you see frequently in Germany, and Betriebsrat refers to a works council.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'blitze', related to 'blicken' (to look, to flash), from Proto-Germanic '*blikjanan' (to shine).
examples: Der Blitz hat in den Baum eingeschlagen. (The lightning struck the tree.)
Der Blitz der Kamera war zu hell. (The camera flash was too bright.)
details.usage: Means both lightning and camera flash, and has been borrowed into English (blitz, blitzkrieg); the idiom 'wie vom Blitz getroffen' means thunderstruck.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bodam' (ground, bottom), from Proto-Germanic '*butmaz', related to English 'bottom'.
examples: Der Boden in der Küche muss geputzt werden. (The floor in the kitchen needs to be cleaned.)
Die Pflanzen wachsen gut in diesem Boden. (The plants grow well in this soil.)
details.usage: Means floor, ground, or soil depending on context, and appears in compounds like Fußboden (floor), Erdboden (ground), and Dachboden (attic).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'brunno' (spring, well), from Proto-Germanic '*brunnō', related to the concept of a water source.
examples: In der Mitte des Platzes steht ein schöner Brunnen. (In the middle of the square there is a beautiful fountain.)
Früher holte man Wasser aus dem Brunnen. (In earlier times, people fetched water from the well.)
details.usage: Means both a well and a fountain; German cities often have decorative Brunnen in public squares, and the idiom 'das Kind ist in den Brunnen gefallen' means the damage is already done.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Buch' (book, from Old High German 'buoh') and 'Handlung' (trade, business), literally a 'book trade' shop.
examples: Die Buchhandlung um die Ecke hat eine gute Auswahl. (The bookshop around the corner has a good selection.)
Ich habe das Buch in einer Buchhandlung bestellt. (I ordered the book at a bookstore.)
details.usage: Germany has a fixed book price law (Buchpreisbindung) that protects independent Buchhandlungen by requiring all retailers to sell books at the publisher's set price.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Old French 'dame' (lady), from Latin 'domina' (mistress of the house), from 'domus' (house).
examples: Meine Damen und Herren, herzlich willkommen! (Ladies and gentlemen, welcome!)
Die Dame an der Rezeption war sehr freundlich. (The lady at the reception was very friendly.)
details.usage: A polite and formal term for a woman or lady, used in addresses like 'Meine Damen und Herren' and on door signs (Damen = Ladies); also the name of the board game checkers (Dame).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'sich erholen' (to recover, to relax), from 'er-' (prefix indicating completion) + 'holen' (to fetch).
examples: Nach der Arbeit brauche ich etwas Erholung. (After work I need some relaxation.)
Der Urlaub war eine gute Erholung. (The vacation was a good rest.)
details.usage: Refers to both physical recovery and recreational rest, and appears in compounds like Erholungsgebiet (recreation area) and Erholungsurlaub (rest vacation).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fest' (firm, fixed), which also developed the meaning of a festive celebration, related to Latin 'festum' (feast).
examples: Wir feiern ein großes Fest im Garten. (We are celebrating a big party in the garden.)
Das Oktoberfest ist das berühmteste Fest in Deutschland. (Oktoberfest is the most famous festival in Germany.)
details.usage: Refers to festivals, parties, and celebrations of all kinds, and appears in compounds like Volksfest (folk festival), Schulfest (school celebration), and the saying 'Man muss die Feste feiern, wie sie fallen' (you should celebrate when the opportunity arises).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Garten' (garden, from Old High German 'garto', related to English 'yard') and 'Arbeit' (work).
examples: Am Wochenende mache ich gerne Gartenarbeit. (On weekends I like to do gardening.)
Die Gartenarbeit im Frühling macht viel Spaß. (Gardening in spring is a lot of fun.)
details.usage: Gardening is one of the most popular hobbies in Germany, connected to the tradition of Schrebergärten (allotment gardens) found in most German cities.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Gast' (guest, from Old High German 'gast') and 'Geber' (giver, from 'geben' = to give), literally 'one who gives to guests'.
examples: Der Gastgeber hat uns herzlich empfangen. (The host welcomed us warmly.)
Deutschland war 2006 Gastgeber der Fußball-WM. (Germany was the host of the 2006 FIFA World Cup.)
details.usage: Used for both a personal host and a host country/city for events; the feminine form is Gastgeberin, and related words include Gastfreundschaft (hospitality).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gidult' (patience, endurance), from 'dulten' (to endure, to tolerate), related to Latin 'tolerare'.
examples: Bitte haben Sie etwas Geduld. (Please have some patience.)
Geduld ist eine wichtige Tugend. (Patience is an important virtue.)
details.usage: An uncountable noun, and the related adjective is 'geduldig' (patient); the expression 'mir reißt die Geduld' means I'm losing my patience.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gerüsche' (noise), derived from 'rauschen' (to rustle, to make noise), with the collective prefix 'ge-'.
examples: Hast du das Geräusch gehört? (Did you hear that noise?)
Das Geräusch kommt aus der Küche. (The noise is coming from the kitchen.)
details.usage: Refers to any sound or noise, and differs from 'Lärm' (loud, disturbing noise) in being more neutral; compounds include Hintergrundgeräusch (background noise) and geräuschlos (noiseless).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Handwerk' (handicraft, from 'Hand' + 'Werk' = hand + work) and the agent suffix '-er'.
examples: Wir brauchen einen Handwerker für die Reparatur. (We need a craftsman for the repair.)
Der Handwerker kommt morgen um 9 Uhr. (The craftsman comes tomorrow at 9 o'clock.)
details.usage: Refers to skilled tradespeople such as plumbers, electricians, and carpenters; Germany's dual education system (Ausbildung) for Handwerker is highly respected and a model for vocational training worldwide.
